column5: Description

Visit a winter roosting site for Bald Eagles located on the Oregon/California border. Harsh weather has provided the Eagles with a much anticipated food supply. Birds who have failed to migrate before the storm are now in a weakened, disadvantaged condition. Watch as Eagles scavenge the marshland for food, and learn why Ben Franklin thought the birds too lazy to be an appropriate symbol of the nation.

Driftnet fishing boats can lay out up to 30 miles of net in a single night. They capture everything that swims by, from commercially useful fish to dolphins, sharks and protected salmon. The result is overfishing of the worldwide stock of seafood.

Archaeological digs are being gutted by thieves looking a quick buck on the black market. Tools, bits of pottery, and petroglyphs are all the clues we have to ancient Oregon residents. In the Columbia River Gorge looting occurs on a weekly basis.
Development and wildlife habitat can work together. The Koll Creekside marsh in Beaverton is a wildlife refuge and a highŌĆōtech industrial site. Portland Oregon
All you need is a chute and the courage to run off a 500 foot high hill with the chute open. Paragliding Willamette Valley Oregon

Portland is one of the first major metropolitan cities to set aside land for a network of urban refuges. Oaks Bottom Wildlife Refuge has already been designated, and activist Mike Houck is busy trying to get as many as 70 other spots on the list.
Experts show us how to spot this deadly disease and what precautions we can take to protect ourselves for the diseaseŌĆōbearing tick. Lyme disease
Smith Rocks State Park is known around the world as a great place to try your hand at gymnastic climbing. Central Oregon rock climbing

SOAR is an organization dedicated to providing outdoor activities for the disabled. We rock climb with a woman who has cerebral palsy. SOAR unites the disabled with expert climbers and other teachers of outdoor skills. We profile three individuals who have the courage to test their limits on the face of Horse Thief Butte.
PEREGRINE FALCON REINTRODUCTION ŌĆōŌĆō Join us for a trip to the Hells Canyon Birds of Prey Area and see efforts to bring the falcon back to Oregon.

They are a popular attraction along the coast unless you're in commercial fishing. We explore the controversy. Seals and sea lions on the Oregon coast are a big draw for tourists, and they're protected by federal law. But commercial fishermen say there are too many seals and sea lions, and that they are eating all the good fish.
We visit a functioning steam mill and meet the man who has run the place for more than 50 years. Willamette Valley Oregon

Summer Lake Wildlife Management Area is located about 70 miles south of La Pine. The refuge is home to thousands of birds, including snow geese, whistling swans, and over 200 other species. It is a flat alkaline marsh that was set aside in 1944 to provide wildlife habitat as well as hunting opportunities for sportsmen.

This massive stretch of swampland surrounding The New River provided critical resources to Native American tribes and early settler traders in the 1850s. For historical and environmental reasons, groups like the Nature Conservancy are pushing to restore what is now pasture land to its natural condition. Ranchers, however, who have owned the property for years, are highly opposed to the idea of giving away their prime grazing land.

A series of streams along the south coast of Oregon used to be home to healthy salmon runs, but those have been declining for decades. Scientists believe the streams in this area are particularly vulnerable to the erosion that results from clear-cut logging.
We take in a little steelhead fishing while learning about efforts to protect the river. Deschutes River Central Oregon boating
The most sophisticated animal forensic lab in the nation is the Fish and Wildlife Department's lab in Ashland. The evidence to convict poachers can be obtained months after an animal is killed.
We look at the efforts of an Oregon woman who organized a statewide beach cleanup that became the model for other states. Oregon Coast
The sport is being attacked because irresponsible mountain biking damages the environment. Experts show us how to ride with as little impact as possible. Mountain biking Portland Oregon
The sport is being attacked because irresponsible mountain biking damages the environment. Experts show us how to ride with as little impact as possible. Mountain biking Portland Oregon

Livelihood of fisherman, highway for sailers, dumping ground for all. The ocean is a vital resource for human civilization, and yet we pollute it with every kind of trash imaginable. Such a vast resource, the ocean used to seem limitless, causing an "out of sight, out of mind" mentality for trash dumping. Now the Port of Newport has set up one of the first sites in the country for recycling garbage once thrown overboard by ocean going vessels.

Along with their forest home, many elk were wiped out by the eruption of Mount St. Helens. Now they are grazing on the new grassy meadows created by collapsing mountain. These closely-watched elk seem to be bouncing back.

Join Mike Wisdom, a US Forest Service biologist as he explains this long-term comprehensive study of elk and deer set in the Blue Mountains near La Grande, Oregon. The study looks at the impact of cattle and rangeland, motorized traffic and roads and timber production on the health of elk and deer herds.

A plaster cast of a footprint first convinced Grover Krantz, a visiting professor of anthropology at Oregon State University, that there really is a bigfoot. Since then he's met over 70 people who claim to have seen Sasquatch, and over half of their stories, he says, are completely credible.

column4: 01.07 In Search of Sasquatch -- Chris Johnson
column5: Chris Johnson lives in rural Stayton, Oregon and spends a lot of time bow hunting in the misty Cascade Mountains. One evening he saw a creature with a furry black face. He returns to the site and tells his story, which is similar to tales of Sasquatch that go back hundreds of years.

It's back to the future for one environmentally-minded logging operation near Ashland. This timber owner cuts trees selectively to improve the health of the forest. He uses horses to haul out the logs because they have less impact on the land than heavy equipment.

Where the Snake River runs through Hells Canyon in northeastern Oregon, a weekly mail boat delivers more than just letters. Some ranchers along the river don't have phones or boats of their own, and the mail boat is their only contact with the outside world. The boat delivers groceries and supplies, and even ferries pets to the vet. In summer tourists come along for the ride, but in winter it's a dangerous ride.
We follow an Eagle Point, Oregon man training for the Iditarod on the Oregon coast then in Alaska, where the conditions are primitive.
We follow an Eagle Point, Oregon man training for the Iditarod on the Oregon coast then in Alaska, where the conditions are primitive.

At the International Landsailing Championship in the California desert, a father and son team from Portland test out their new land yacht. While traditional land yachts look like windsurfing boards with wheels, theirs has a solid metal wing in place of a cloth sail.

Gray skies, windy weather, and cold water do not typically draw flocks of people to the beach. With a pure love of surfing, however, these hearty groups of men are willing to brave the elements in order to enjoy the waves of the Oregon Coast. They may not be cheered on by sun bathers in bikinis, but the swells themselves are enough to keep Oregon surfers coming back for more.

We travel 5,000 feet under the Pacific Ocean for a look at research into volcanic activity past and present. The seismic forces that shaped Oregon's coast are still alive miles offshore, where an underwater mountain range remains a volcanic hotspot.
They eat, sleep and drill just like the horse soldiers of the 1870s. This group of men make history come alive, right down to the brass buttons.
A rock gym in Southeast Portland makes it possible for rock climbers to practice at lunch or after work. It's also a great way for beginners to practice rock climbing in a controlled environment.
A rather unusual research project scares hatchery salmon, which lead a rather sheltered life, to prepare them for their release and the dangers they'll face in the open ocean.
Dave Siddon runs Wildlife Images, an animal care compound that provides medical care and shelter for wounded and orphaned animals. The compound is home to everything from a vulture to a cougar.
Field Guide heads to a remote, unmarked spot overlooking Lake Billy Chinook to find a geologic marvel. Covering a hillside, huge rock pillars are topped by a series of large balancing rocks.
Biologists say sea urchin divers are having a big impact on the sea lions, an endangered species. Urchins provide a fishery meeting demands for exotic seafood in places like Japan.

A right whale off the coast of Newfoundland is tagged with a radio transmitter, and a biologist from the Marine Science Center in Newport is able to track the whale's movements on his computer screen. Satellite technology is opening up whole new worlds of information about whales, their travels and the depth of their dives.

Scientists discover that a marsh adjacent to Woodburn High School is an ancient peat bog. The bog has the potential to hold a treasure trove of fossilized bones from animals of the Ice Age, including camels, saber tooth tigers and giant wolves.

Since the 1970s, the population of western Canada geese in the Lower Columbia has been increasing. The eruption of Mount St. Helens created a new island, Fisher Island, that is a goose haven, but the Port of Portland is considering a dredging project that would destroy that habitat.

The noble men and women of the Portland Mountain Rescue team patrol the steepest, roughest parts of Mt. Hood on foot. Follow the team up the mountain to the summit and witness just how dedicated the volunteers of the rescue team are. Learn why these superb winter hikers do what they do and what procedures they use when disaster strikes.

With increased pressure from predators and a loss of habitat caused by the introduction of European beach grass in the 1930's, the numbers of Western Snowy Plovers on the Oregon Coast have been in decline in recent years. In Coos Bay, developers and wildlife managers try to find a balance between economic growth and habitat restoration. We examine the battle to protect this sandpiper-like bird that is now listed as endangered in Oregon.

The bat is one of the most common creatures on earth and yet we still don't know much about it. An Dr. Steven Cross of Southern Oregon University wants to change that. Join us underground and learn a lot more about the Townsend's Big Eared Bat. These bats emerge late in the evening to forage and are swift, highly maneuverable fliers. Prey items include small moths, flies, lacewings, dung beetles, and sawflies.

Rick Westcott turned his childhood obsession with insects into a career as an entomologist with the Oregon Department of agriculture. Join Dr. Westcott out in the field as well as in his Salem office as he catalogs Oregon's many lesser known residents. A colorful character with extensive knowledge Rick is a joy to listen to and learn from as he gives us a tour of Oregon's state insect collection.

Many of Oregon's pictograph and petroglyph sites have damaged by by vandalism. At risk is art that has historical and mystical significance. Artist Greg Bettis has made a career of recording and restoring Northwest Native American rock art. He is a passionate advocate for preserving and protecting this resource.

Join Oregon Field Guide's Jim Newman and the BLM's Jerry Hubbard in a tour of the remote Owyhee Uplands near Jordan Valley in southeastern Oregon. They find a thunder egg, or geode, as they hike to the top of Three Fingers Rock. From the summit they take in views of wild horses below, and Mahogany Mountain and Leslie Gulch in the distance. Then they visit the scenic Painted Canyon on their return trip.

Join Oregon Field Guide's Jim Newman and the BLM's Jerry Hubbard in a tour of the remote Owyhee Uplands near Jordan Valley in southeastern Oregon. They find a thunder egg, or geode, as they hike to the top of Three Fingers Rock. From the summit they take in views of wild horses below, and Mahogany Mountain and Leslie Gulch in the distance. Then they visit the scenic Painted Canyon on their return trip.

This remote section of southeastern Oregon is virtually unknown to most of us. You'll tour the geological treasures of the Jordan Craters, Leslie Gulch and the Owyhee uplands in this halfŌĆōhour special report.

A cowboy can do a little bit of everything, but a buckaroo sticks to tradition. That's the opinion of one Harney County buckaroo who rides out every day in silvers spurs, with an old-fashioned vest and a scarf around his neck. A buckaroo prefers a horse to a pickup truck, and he's highly skilled in roping, riding and branding. The word buckaroo comes from the Spanish vaqueros who were the first cattlemen in Oregon.
Dick and Patty Jenkins run the Barton Lake Ranch near Burns. They're cattle ranchers, but to cut back on the price of hiring cowboys, they use a helicopter to track and herd their cattle.

Ranchers in Lane County loose valuable livestock every spring when coyote packs swell with new pups. Coyotes cost farmers hundreds of dollars a year in sheep and other small livestock. To give local farms some much-needed relief government payed trappers have been employed to catch these troublesome predators. The expense of the program however, and the humaneness of the trapping, has sparked a debate about issues of predator control.

At a ranch near Umatilla, 300 buffalo graze the land. They're being raised for meat and to be sold as breed stock to other buffalo ranchers. They're easy to care for and their meat is healthier than beef.

Foreign ships entering Coos Bay carry microscopic stowaways in their ballast water that could impact Oregon's coastal ecology. Dr. Jonathan Geller of the Oregon Institute of Marine Biology studies the water in these ships looking for the larvae and unhatched eggs of non-native species in an effort to prevent the establishment of new colonies in the bay.

Join the Oregon Department of Fish and Wildlife as it stocks a couple of high mountain lakes via helicopter. Around 400 lakes are stocked with 200,000 trout each year, keeping anglers happy. The department continues to explore new options for stocking the lakes. However, fish are not native to these high mountain lakes and the National Parks System is currently doing a study on the impact of the fish on native frogs and salamanders.
They're an introduced species in Oregon and have been getting a bum rap for years. Find out the truth about possums.
The hatchery program at the Dexter Dam is so successful that there is a glut of salmon. There is some controversy over the handling of these salmon by the Army Corps of Engineers.
A new fad for precision stunt kites had grown men practicing kite flying as a team. We travel with one team to the stunt kite Grand Nationals in Seaside, Oregon.

The Tualatin River was a commercial waterway and later, a site for thousands of swimmers and boaters every summer. Then companies began dumping sewage into the river, and it became the most polluted river in Oregon. Now it's the object of a multi-million dollar cleanup project.

On the coast of Oregon lives the darlingtonia, also known as a pitcher or cobra plant. This meat-eating plant is specially designed to capture its insect prey. The best place to see these bizarre plants is at the Darlingtonia Wayside near Florence.

In 1990, the research ship Aloha set off to explore the sea bed off the coast of Gold Beach on the South Coast. The trip was meant to determine whether there were commercially valuable deposits of minerals, including gold, platinum and titanium, off the coast. Unexpectedly, Greenpeace's Rainbow Warrior and a flotilla of local fishermen surrounded the boat to protest offshore mining. The expedition failed to find significant mineral deposits.
a report on recent finds in Oregon and the University of Oregon's collection. University of oregon

Oregon's Blacktail deer are elusive and secretive. You would be lucky to see one during daylight hours, and that's why Fish and Game officials have adopted spotlight counting, during nighttime hours when the deer are at their most active. Learn more about the great Tilamook Brun which devastated the coastal forest, but was a boom the the Blacktail population. As the Forest has grown the Blacktails numbers have declined.

How do you capture a bighorn sheep? Fire a net from a gun while hanging out of an airborne helicopter. Why? Bighorns were common in Oregon until sheep ranching and hunting wiped them out. Since the 1950s, the state of Oregon has been reseeding the sheep across the state. The Hart Mountain Antelope Refuge is home to the main herd, which was originally transported from Canada.

Hikers and the Forest Service get together to create T.R.I.S., the Trails Information System for computer. Information includes current conditions and difficulty level for more than 400 trails in Oregon and Washington.

Take a tour of the rugged Steens Mountains and surrounding area. Listen to scientists talk about the collection of information, examining pollen and ash in sedimentary rock and core samples to unearth records of climatic and geologic changes going back thousands of years.
Groups participate in team-building activities on various ground level and high wire courses at the Sunriver Challenge Course outside of Bend, Oregon.

Many species of animals that thrived in Oregon 200 years ago are now gone. A distinct species of Oregon buffalo was hunted to extinction before Europeans even arrived. Next up was the sea otter, eliminated by Russian fur traders. Then went the wolves, grizzlies, condors, fishers, lynxes, and other victims of settlement.

The land surrounding Mount St. Helens is some of the newest in the world. A moonscape typography created by an explosive volcanic blast, little plant and wildlife was left unscathed. Special vegetation planted after the blast however, has provided an unintentionally abundant food supply for a once dwindling species of elk on the mountain. Now making an unexpected comeback, biologists monitor the growth of the population to ensure their survival.

Visit a winter roosting site for Bald Eagles located on the Oregon/California border. Harsh weather has provided the Eagles with a much anticipated food supply. Birds who have failed to migrate before the storm are now in a weakened, disadvantaged condition. Watch as Eagles scavenge the marshland for food, and learn why Ben Franklin thought the birds too lazy to be an appropriate symbol of the nation.

When a forest fire breaks out, the response is rapid. A small city springs up almost overnight in the vicinity. When 1500 firefighters and crew descend on a fire in Grant County, their camp becomes the second biggest city in the county.

Rapid changes in temperature could potentially cause droughts, heat waves, and floods across Oregon. Increases in green house gases may have happened before in the Earthšs history, but never at the dramatic rate that scientists are currently studying. Scientists hope to learn more about these changes in order to predict, or even prevent, the possible outcomes. See how some University of Oregon scientists are using sophisticated methods of examining past climate in order to predict what the future climate might be like. Changes seem to be happening at a much faster rate than anything wešve seen in the past and there are indications that Oregonšs temperatures may rise 10 degrees over the next century. The big challenge is to predict the change before it gets here and determine our capacity to deal with the consequences of increasing drought, floods and heatwaves.

Trappers in Oregon catch coyotes, fox, bobcat, muskrat, beaver and other animals and sell their pelts. Animal rights activists deplore trapping which they say is cruel. But the state relies on trappers to keep populations in check.

Once considering the cougar a dangerous varmint, human kind now appears to be willing to reach an accommodation with the fierce carnivore. But before an understanding can be reached, Biologists must learn as much as they can about the mountain lion. Head into the snow-covered mountains as researchers track down cougars with deceptive ease using radio collars, and watch as they tranquillize a mother and her cub in order to study their behavior.

Almost disappearing entirely from the state, Peregrine Falcons are struggling to make a comeback in Oregon. The use of a chemical for bug control in the 40's and 50's called DDT, caused an unintended decimation of the Peregrine population. Falcons who ate the bugs began laying eggs with soft shells that never hatched. Biologists since have been breeding the birds and releasing the chicks into the wild. Will their efforts make a difference?

Better logging practices are helping more birds survive in the Mount Hood National Forest. Many birds nest inside dead trees, which loggers now leave behind. Northern flickers and hairy woodpeckers excavate cavities in the wood that they use for a season, and then abandon, to the benefit of other birds.
The Rogue River flows 215 miles from Crater Lake to the Pacific Ocean. It's a beautiful trip by raft down one of the first rivers protected under the national Wild and Scenic Rivers bill.

In rails to trails programs across the state, abandoned railroad tracks are removed and the right-of-ways lines converted into wide, even walking trails. Field Guide follows the creation of part of Portland's Springwater Trail.

The Cook family farm is called Bird Haven, and it's a nesting site for several types of swallows. The birds eat insects from the farm's peach and cherry trees, eliminating the need for pesticides. Unfortunately, fruit-eating birds are now the problem.

In spring in the high desert country, the male sage grouse puts on a show with a courting ritual that involves puffing up two sacks of air in his fluffy chest and spreading his tail feathers. But when the hens go to nest, very few of their chicks survive. We follow an Oregon State University scientist who is studying the hens, and looking for ways to reverse the decline in sage grouse populations.
The Pacific Flyway Project is Oregon's first attempt to gauge the health of the migratory shore bird population. The work on the ground is being done by volunteer birdwatchers.

Gill net fishermen in the Columbia River catch chinook salmon headed for number of tributaries. Some of these stocks are far more depleted than others. Now scientists can use genetic testing to determine the tributary of origin for fish caught in the Columbia.

Students and archeologists find relics of a set of houses that were built in Hells Canyon over 1,000 years ago. The ancestors of today's Nez Perce Indians spent their winters here, living off the land.

We travel to Diamond Valley in Southwestern Oregon to capture extremely rare footage of the wild Kigers mustangs, believed to be descendants of the Spanish mustangs. Thought to be extinct for a hundred years, these small, elusive horses were rediscovered in the 1970s. As wild and wary as deer or antelope, these horses live in an isolation that preserves their Spanish bloodline.

Join us on a five-day odyssey down the wild Owyhee River and see stunning attractions like the monuments of Chalk Basin Montgomery Gorge, spectacularly eroded cliffs reminiscent of ancient Rome, and even the site of a nineteenth century shootout.

Decades of Forest Service fire suppression have created dangerous levels of flammable undergrowth. Controlled burns are starting to be used more and more as a valuable tool in preventing future catastrophic forest fires.
The wind has to be just right for hang glider pilots to fly, alone and with no motors or navigational gear. A hang gliding meet in Oceanside brings together a group of experienced pilots.

Starting near the town of Fields and ending in Denio Creek, the Field Guide crew joins some members of the Oregon Desert Trail Association for a grueling hike through the high desert of the Pueblo Mountains. Beautiful vistas and wild flowers abound on this four day hike through Oregon's rugged high desert country.
In Seaside, Ron Pittard creates amazingly lifelike replicas so anglers can have a trophy without taxidermy. In fact, many are trophies of fish that were caught and released.

Salmon in the Willamette River are counted visually as they pass Willamette Falls on their way upstream to their spawning grounds. Scientists find that forty percent of those salmon don't make it all the way upstream. To find out why, they attach radio transmitters to a sampling of the salmon.

The Warner sucker is a small fish that lives only in the Warner Basin of southeastern Oregon. With its habitat drying up, the fish is struggling to survive. The introduction of non-native game fish in the 1970s added to the fish's troubles.

Annual burning works well to clear and sterilize fields after grass seed has been harvested. Unfortunately, it pumps tons of pollution into the air. A scientist thinks composting is the wave of the future.

Join Mike Wisdom, a US Forest Service biologist as he explains this long-term comprehensive study of elk and deer set in the Blue Mountains near La Grande, Oregon. The study looks at the impact of cattle and rangeland, motorized traffic and roads and timber production on the health of elk and deer heards.

Among the alpine meadows of the Columbia River Gorge are clues to the Indian culture that flourished for thousands of years. In prehistoric times, residents left evidence of their culture, including strange rock shelters or pits in rockfalls.
Lost Lake is a recreational spot at the foot of Mount Hood that is no longer a secret. The area is under pressure from overuse, timber cuts, and hotels moving into the area.

A beautiful look at the scenery and wildlife of the Malheur National Wildlife Refuge, an oasis in the desert of southeastern Oregon. We hear the wind as well as bird calls as we look at photographs of beautiful cloud formations, a gorgeous sunset, and peaceful marshes that are home to such species as white pelican, red winged blackbird, and northern harrier.

Demand for Oregon oysters outstrips supply. Scientists are hoping that the hardy, tasty suminoe oyster, from Japan, can give a boost to the oyster farming industry. The oysters are being studied at the Hatfield Marine Science Center and in the waters of Yaquina Bay.

It's a day on the river, with a professional salmon fishing guide, looking for spring chinook. But instead of trees and mountains, the backdrop is bridges and skyscrapers, because the fish are biting in the Willamette near downtown Portland's Waterfront Park.

Hikers take time out for a relaxing dip in a rustic hot springs-fed pool at the edge of the Alvord Desert, in Harney County. All the owner asks is that you don't vandalize the site and that you leave him alone.

Trumpeter swans were once thought to have been hunted to extinction. Now a small population in Idaho and Wyoming is growing. Can some of those birds be transplanted back into a historic wintering spot in south central Oregon?

Photographers have been coming to the Photographers Horse Round up for years. Just north of Bend, Oregon you'll find Rock Springs Guest Ranch home of the Horse Round Up. Renowned photographer Wilma Roberts started the Round Up in the 1960's. Since than photographers from all over the world have taken part in this unique seminar. The combination of horses, dust and light combine to make images which seem to come straight out of the old west.

Cattle ranchers on private land working to restore the environment and improve grazing at the same time. Doc Hatfield is a pioneering cattle rancher who is trying to reverse a century of overgrazing on his land and bring back green grass.

The volcanic peaks of central Oregon are the perfect location for an observatory that may provide new data about deep space. The Pine Mountain Observatory, 6300 feet above sea level, uses relatively small telescopes with enormous computing power.

Take a leisurely seven mile hike and see one of the greatest collections of waterfalls in Oregon. There are ten falls in the park, including six that are over 100 feet tall. Several of the falls have amphitheaters behind them that allow the hiker to walk behind the falls. The park also has a large conference center, horse corrals, and four miles of bicycle trails.

Taxol is an anti-cancer medication with a lot of promise. It comes only from the Pacific yew tree which grows only in the Pacific Northwest. While the trees are scarce and grow extremely slowly, timber companies continue to toss the trees away when clear cutting.
A Closeup look at this amazing animal. Is it a vicious predator or a misunderstood canine?

After the eruption of Mount St. Helens, non-native grass was planted to prevent erosion. Scientists worry that seeding was a mistake, as erosion could have actually aided the recovery process, uncovering native species that survived the blast. A once in a life time opportunity to study the process of natural restoration, Scientists fear that human intervention has forever altered the face of Mount St. Helens' recovering ecosystem.

Squawfish are devouring endangered Salmon smolt, and now there's a bounty on them in an attempt to protect the smolt heading downstream. The Squawfish population has soared along the many hydroelectric dams which cross the mighty Columbia River. Biologist, researchers and fisheries managers are all studying the problem, and have come up with some innovative ways to catch and use the Squawfish.

The U.S. Coast Guard sends its students from all over the country to train in the waters at the mouth of the Columbia River. In a place known as the "graveyard of the Pacific," they must maneuver a 44-foot boat and retrieve a mannequin from the giant waves.
A huge increase in the number of rafters and other recreators on the Deschutes River leads to conflict. Folks disagree on whether restrictions are needed.

Portland's waterfront was once covered in warehouses and docks. The creation of Tom McCall Waterfront Park changed the face of the city, and it is now so well-loved and well-used that the city is struggling to keep it in shape.

Nationally acclaimed bird-call recorder Jeff Keller sets out to capture the best audio recording of the Sandhill Crane ever made. Setting up his equipment overnight and waiting into the morning for the cranes to arrive, the birds must land within 100 feet of his microphones in order for him to get a quality recording. With each approaching flock of birds flying right overhead and out of range, will Jeff get a recording of the cranes at all?

At the Oregon Caves National Monument, painstaking restoration work is performed by hand. When the caves became popular with tourists earlier in the 20th century, "improvements" like widened passageways and added staircases caused unintentional damage to the caves' fragile environment.
A Native American family's attempts to keep their grandchildren interested in the old ways. NE Oregon

During the Oregon gold rush of the late 1800s Granite, 40 miles from Baker City, thrived with a population of 2,000. Now only a handful of residents remain. Jerry Myers gives us a tour of the cemetery and town buildings. His tour includes stories of residents of the town that were told to him by his Uncle Oat, resident of Granite from 1920 to 1978. Uncle Oat is known for being the sole resident of Granite in the 1950s.
A look at the work that goes into setting up a world class aquariumŌĆōŌĆōincluding an ocean roundup for native fish.Newport Oregon Coast Aquaruium
As close as it is to the Portland Metro area, the Sandy River remains pristine, a crucial habitat for salmon and a source of drinking water. But new development is threatening this waterway.

Scientists are just beginning to learn about the life cycle of the elusive marbled murrelet. These sea birds nest at least 150 feet off the ground in old growth trees, and they are suffering from a loss of habitat.

Commonly seen in waterways around Oregon, Nutria are a non-native species that originally came from South America. They were introduced to the state in the 1950's to be bred and raised for their fur. Some breeders released their stock into the wild, creating problems for farmers and native habitat that continue to this day.
The North American Truffling Society heads out into the Oregon woods to hunt for truffles. These fungi, prized in the culinary world, grow just under surface on the forest floor.

After the turn of the century deteriorating habitats, overfishing, competition from other species, and even intentional killing has decimated the once plentiful bull trout population. As fish eaters with a "drab" name, scientists reason that the Bull Trout is a misunderstood and under-appreciated species of fish in Oregon. By implanting transmitting devices in the fish, scientists hope to help the bull trout by learning more about them.

Travel to Oregon's high desert country for a look at the vast wildlife refuge. The Hart Mountain Antelope Refuge was established in 1936 as a sanctuary for the pronghorn antelope. The land around Hart Mountain is also home to some 330 other species of wildlife.

Get a rare glimpse behind the scenes as we follow the Alexander family as they compete at the Oregon State Fair with their pigs. Learn about the rewards of cooperation and discipline in raising and caring for the animals.

The rugged Crooked River Gorge near Bend attracts nature-lovers because it is so remote. But that same remoteness makes it tempting to people who want to dump garbage. A group of volunteers come to clean up the gorge, hiking in and in some cases climbing and rappelling down cliffs.

Oregon's only saltwater lake is a desolate place that is a popular stopover for migratory waterfowl. It's located near Valley Falls in south central Oregon. The Chewaucan River flows into Abert Lake, but no water flows out, so minerals concentrate in the lake. The water is very salty and alkaline but supports a population of brine shrimp. It is also the work site of a man who earns his living catching and selling brine shrimp.

Every year over 27,000 visitors take the trip to the 8,000 foot top of Mt. Howard in the scenic Wallowa Mountains near Joseph, OR. The tram has one of the steepest vertical ascents in the U.S. and has been running for over 20 years. Maintenance of the 27,000 tons of cable is vital. Follow a crew of workers as it looks for worn out parts high above the tree tops. Appreciate the exquisite beauty of these mountains in the northeast corner of Oregon.

Many species of amphibians such as the spotted and cascade frogs and the western toad are disappearing in Oregon and may soon be extinct. Scientists suspect that environmental factors, such as the depleted ozone layer and higher levels of uv radiation may be contributing to their decline.

Despite cold water and slippery rocks, hard souls submerge themselves in pounding waterfalls to capture Lamprey eels at the base of the Willamette Falls. Although the number of Lamprey eels has been declining over the years, it's not because of people like the Scott Brothers or the members of Native American tribes who capture them. Similar to Salmon Lamprey require pristine streams in order to spawn, which are becoming harder and harder to find.

Huge tankers now travel up the Columbia River from the Pacific Ocean. Fifty years ago and more, small fishing vessels with sails were the norm. Bits of abandoned townsites and other pieces of history dot the river banks.
A photo club heads out to the Columbia River Gorge to capture some natural beauty. Along the way, they offer some tips on how to get your photos of waterfalls and flowers looking their best.
Travel underwater to learn a lot about the Western Pond TurtleŌĆōŌĆōa creature seldom seen in its natural habitat. Southern Oregon
Wendell Harmon is a timber owner and logger who believes in partial cutting. His forests remain full of trees as he selectively logs his holdings. It works, but it requires patience and planning.
Retrieving dogs like labs can be taught complicated tasks to assist their owners in duck hunting. They compete in a retrieving contest, and trainers offer tips on how to get a family pet to behave.

When Malheur Lake flooded, the burial sites of prehistoric Paiute Indians were dislodged. Now the Indians of the Burns Paiute Tribe pay tribute to their ancestors and rebury the bones in the Malheur Wildlife Refuge.
Believe it or not, good old fashioned snow shoeing is growing in popularity. It's simple and easy, and appeals to hikers. We follow a group of beginners on the slopes of Mount Hood.

At one time, the houseboats along the Columbia and Willamette rivers were affordable, funky and off the radar. New technology and a rising value placed on river views is threatening the old houseboat way of life.
A small pond near Bend is maintained for outdoor ice skating, although some residents find the going a little rough, at least at first.

Cattle ranchers on private land working to restore the environment and improve grazing at the same time. Doc Hatfield is a pioneering cattle rancher who is trying to reverse a century of overgrazing on his land and bring back green grass.
Tugboats move huge barges up and down the Columbia River. Field Guide rides along with a load of grain and meets the pilots who have this unusual and beautiful place to work.

Predators, especially coyotes, are always a problem for sheep herders. An experimental program is teaming ranchers up with specially bred and trained dogs to serve as an alternative to trapping, poisoning or shooting of predators.

Pheasants are not native to North American, but hunters are fond of them. Ringneck pheasants used to roam the Willamette Valley, but changing farm and forest conditions have reduced their numbers. The Fish and Game Department is testing a theory that Szechwan pheasants from China will fare better and generate more income from hunting licenses.

Geologists say that Oregon has a history of catastrophic earthquakes. A special half-hour report looks at proof of a shaky past and the potential for another major earthquake in the Pacific Northwest. We also look at what's being done to increase earthquake safety.

By building dams and digging channels, beavers have a major impact on the waterways where they live. For farmers and ranchers, they can be a real pest. Many are killed, but some are relocated to wild areas where their skills benefit the environment.
Whitewater rafting has exploded in popularity. Each May, top rafters enter the races sponsored by the Northwest Rafters Association on the Clackmas river.

A flood in the 1980s unearthed several ancient grave sites near Malheur Lake in southeast Oregon. The Burns/Paiute Tribe allowed limited study of the remains, and scientists found new information about what life was like in prehistoric Oregon.

Revisit the ice age with Oregon Field Guide to uncover prehistoric secrets about the coexistence of humans and mastodons. We search for petroglyphs in Southeastern Oregon, with supposed elephant-like figures carved into them. The trek is long, through miles of lonesome country, and with darkness quickly approaching we wonder if we'll get to see this mastodon petroglyphs at all.
A small but dedicated group of scuba divers explore the cold waters of the Oregon coast. The water near Cape Lookout and Three Arch Rocks is full of marine life.

Renewable energy specialist Don Bain takes us to 7 mile hill outside of the Dalles, and explains the potential of this area for wind turbines. Choice wind locations are plentiful in Oregon, but convincing the public to tolerate the visual impact of turbine fields is another issue. The potential problems of wind power are solvable, Bain says, and the benefits far out way the negatives.

He's worked on assignment in jungles all over the world, but nature photographer Gerry Ellis loves Oregon's temperate rainforest. He's based in Portland, and he heads out to Mount Hood to explain why he thinks his own back yard is so special.
Upper Klamath Lake is disappearing. There is no longer enough water in the Klamath Basin to please everyone, and farmers, fish, American Indians and migrating birds are all suffering.

An inądepth look at the people who abuse the 1872 Mining Law by building rental homes, dumping trash, disfiguring the countryside and locking the public out of public land.     Also: Cyanide heapąleach mining, which is coming to eastern Oregon. Gold Mining

The coast has been invaded by an ornamental shrub that's gone wild. Gorse, a non-native bush introduced from England, has taken over the dunes of the Oregon coast. The plant's aggressive growth, and seeds that can remain dormant for 30 years has allowed it to outlast all human efforts to control it's spread. Scientists have hope that a spider mite from New Zealand can finally provide a solution.

Salem resident Augusta Rockafeller loves ferns. She shows off her extensive garden planted with native flowers, shrubs and trees. Then it's on to other gardeners who have "gone native," including volunteers with the state's Naturescaping program to encourage native plantings.

Peter Byrne, a resident of Parkdale, Oregon, was once a big game hunter in Nepal. Now dedicated to conservation, he returns to Nepal in search of an elephant he first encountered in the 1950s. The elephant, named Tula Hatti, is one of a dwindling herd of wild Asian elephants, and, measuring 11 feet at the shoulder, is probably the largest Asian elephant in the world.

Take a hike with leader Mary Vogel through some of the most dramatic and beautiful ancient forests in Oregon. The first day is a hike along the South Breitenbush trail among Douglas firs, hemlocks, and cedars. Day two is an eastside hike through ponderosa pines along the Metolius River. The third day is a hike to see Alaskan yellow cedars, a Douglas fir over 700 years old, and a beaver pond.

Entomologist Jim McIver takes us to Prineville where we discover some of the secrets of thatch ants. Their name refers to the thatch-like colony where they live. These ants also tend to aphids, who provide them with honeydew, and collect other food from the sagebrush terrain.

The Klamath Forest National Wildlife Refuge was recently expanded to twice its size, but the new acreage needs to be restored to a more natural state. An international group called the Earth Stewards Network brings young people from around the world to help with work at the refuge.

The Bureau of Land Management has created a backcountry byway tour of Christmas Valley. Field Guide visits Hole-In-The-Ground, Fort Rock, Fremont, Fort Rock Cave, the Devil's Garden Lava Beds and Derrick Cave.

At the edge of the Umpqua National Forest a man is building a home out of trash - yew wood pulled from logging slash piles. A one of a kind recycling project is taking place just west of Glide Oregon. The Field Guide crew takes you on a tour of this unique house and one man's incredible story.
The value of clean, healthy urban streams is being appreciated more and more. Efforts are under way to restore Johnson Creek and other creeks in the Portland Metro area.

Field Guide tours the Bureau of Land Management's backcountry byway in Christmas Valley. The roads are rough, but the rewards include sites like Crack-In-The-Ground, the Dunes Wilderness Study Area, the Lost Forest and Fossil Lake.
On a cold, rainy spring day, kayakers flock to whitewater rapids. It's a wild ride as kayakers compete in many different events of the Bob's Hole Rodeo along the upper Clackamas River.

A spirit of cooperation is helping to restore salmon to the Umatilla River. Generations ago, dams began diverting water from the river to irrigate desert farms. So much water was lost that the native salmon-based economy was totally destroyed. Now water and salmon are returning.

In central Oregon, water drips down through freezing cold caves, creating ice sculptures and frozen lakes. Cave enthusiasts lead the way and explain how important it is to leave the caves just as you find them.

John Zevely looks like your average Oregonian, that is until you see him shooting down the side of Mt. Hood at speeds over 100 miles per hour. Spend the day on the slopes with John and other members of Oregon's growing speed skiing community. Most of the skiers started out alpine skiing, but the adrenaline and sounds of speed skiing have tempted both their friends and family to either join them in the sport, or cheer them on from the side.

On rocks and in small pools only visible during low tide, a rich diversity of life lives on the fringes of the Oregon Coast. although these strange creatures can stand the pummeling of the waves, they are extremely susceptible to damage from human contact.

Commercial growers and hobby gardeners agree: Canby, Oregon, is a great place to grow things. A combination of good soil, good weather, and neighborly support help the ladies of the Maxberg Garden Club and other home gardeners to fill their yards with flowers.

Join members of Oregon's Department of Fish and Wildlife as they sponsor bird hunts for juvenile Oregonians. These hunts serve two purposes first to get children interested in the great outdoors, but more importantly Fish and Wildlife hopes to create Oregon's next wave of hunters. Most people are unaware that a majority of the Departments funds come from revenue generated from hunting fees.

About 400 times a year, search and rescue teams are deployed in Oregon. Most folks who get into trouble get lost in the snow, where the number one danger is death from hypothermia. Experts explain how to avoid falling victim to the cold.

Central Oregon's John Day Fossil Beds National Monument contain a wealth of fossils from plants and animals that are millions of years old. But once the shifting ground exposes a fossil to the open air, researchers have only a few years to find and preserve it before it decomposes.
Logging companies and environmentalists join forces to bring coho salmon back to one of Oregon's coastal creeks.

Every Fourth of July, volunteers across North America head out with nets to count the local butterflies. In the Metolius Basin, children and adults capture an array of colorful winged creatures, then let them go again. The information is used by the Forest Service and other agencies.

Farmers are experimenting with a new computer-aided system to make their use of agricultural chemicals more efficient. They use aerial infrared photography and computers to the analyze soil and the farm equipment is also computer-assisted.

Mt. Jefferson has a bad reputation of being the most difficult volcano to climb in Oregon. Join our team along as we take on the dangerous journey to the summit, and witness the total isolationism that so many climbers with independent spirits seek. With passages like the so-called "death route", huge jagged rocks, and unexpected fogs, find out why climbers put themselves in harms way to summit Mt. Jefferson.
For some, the canoe is a sedate way to paddle across a lake. But the whitewater rapids of Oregon's spring rivers are more to the taste of the Lower Columbia Canoe Club.

Even though plutonium production ceased in 1989, the reservation still has millions of gallons of high-level radioactive waste buried in aging tanks. This one-hour documentary gives viewers an opportunity to see sites at the Hanford Nuclear Reservation that were previously off limits, and looks at federal options to cleanup the site, safety issues, and cost to the public.
We head to beautiful Willapa Bay in Washington with a group of sea kayakers and find out why they love this sport. Sea Kayaking
For their owners, a wolf-dog hybrid is a wonderful animal. But for others, there are compelling reasons to outlaw the ownership and breeding of hybrids.

Wolves flourished in Oregon until they were trapped for fur and hunted as pests. The last wild wolves in Oregon were seen in the 1940s. Environmentalists are excited about the idea of reintroducing wolves to the state, but ranchers are opposed.
This is a leisurely float down one of Oregon's hidden treasures. Eastern Oregon

Turtle populations are declining around the world. The Northwest Ecological Research Institute's Turtle Project is the state's largest turtle clinic, rehabbing sick and injured turtles so they can be returned to the wild. The clinic accepts all turtles, including the threatened Western pond turtle.
Wild, abandoned house cats are not only breeding out-of-control, but they're also responsible for the deaths of millions of birds.

It's like a miniature helicopter, but the flying mechanism works differently. The gyrocopter is an oddity of the sky, beloved by a handful of hobbyists in Oregon, who gather monthly at the Aurora air strip.

Topping trees - that is, cutting them off bluntly across their highest branches - is common in urban areas. Experts increasingly recommend against the practice, and recommend more healthy, natural ways to prune trees.

Wait until you see the beauty of the Alvord Desert from the seat of an ultra light plane! Join recreational pilots from all over the pacific northwest at the annual Alvord Desert Ultra Light Fly-in, and enjoy the view of the "slow, stop-and-smell-the-roses flying" that the ultra light pilots have come to love. Safety is at the forefront of pilots' minds this particular weekend, but the thrill of the flight outweighs their fears of any danger.

Most of the rights to the water in our rivers and streams were given away to settlers before the turn of the century in order to turn worthless desert into farmland. But now more and more people want to put a stop to these irrigation diversions.

The only truly aquatic song bird, the American dipper or water ouzel lives near fast-moving streams in the Pacific Northwest. An expert explains how they use their wings to manoever underwater and some of their other unusual adaptations.

Harney County, Oregon covers 10,180 square miles, but only about seven thousand people live within its boundaries. One can travel along a main highway for miles without seeing a house or fence, just an occasional road that leads seemingly nowhere. The discovery of gold in the early 1860s brought thousands of prospectors through the area. Cattle ranchers, attracted by the vast amount of bunchgrass soon began moving their herds into the region.

Biologists struggle through rugged, snowy terrain in search of a female black bear. Even though she's wearing a radio collar, she's hard to find. This mother bear, with two cubs, is part of a five-year study of the health of the bear population in the Cascades. The scientists literally crawl into her den to check up on her.

Mineral build up with the consistency of corroded concrete creates a natural bowl on the Alvord desert floor holding a very special and interesting lake in place. With temperatures upwards of 100 degrees and water concentrated with arsenic and lead, Borax Lake is a chemical soup uninhabitable by most types of aquatic life. Meet a tiny fish, the Borax Lake Chub, that has managed to survive in these incredibly harsh conditions.

Logging by helicopter helps to preserve and protect land and soil from destruction that would normally be caused by the use of heavy equipment in traditional logging. The 31,000 acre Cook Canyon site near Klamath, Oregon has been an area valued for its spiritual aspects. The logging operation is expensive and has a high potential for danger and must be run efficiently and with a heightened awareness of the workers.

Now in his nineties, John Scharff talks about the history of the Malheur Wildlife Refuge, which he managed from its founding in 1935 until 1971. He and his wife Florence Scharff recount the history of the area, where old homesteads crumble as wildlife returns.

The US Army Corps of Engineers and scientists work with models of our Pacific northwest dams in order to find out how best to improve migration of our salmon runs and protect juveniles from predators as they are released into the river. OFG travels to Vicksburg, MS where the models have been set up and captures the experiments at work.
With a new guide, Field Guide returns to the Owyhee Reservoir in search of rumored petroglyphs depicting long-extinct mastodons.

The Oregon Museum of Science and Industry sponsors Salmon Camp, a special summer camp for American Indian kids. They talk about the historic importance of salmon for Oregon tribes, but also teach the kids career skills in the management and protection of salmon.

Learn about the process of blowing up trees in order to create habitat for wildlife. Logging and the clearing of the logged areas have left little for animals and birds that require snags and hollowed out tree trunks for their survival.

Jean Bucknam is an experienced rock hound who searches for special rocks from the geologically rich area around Prineville. She's helping the state identify good spots to make into public rock collecting areas. She offers tips on hounding and responsible collecting.

Follow the Oregon Field Guide crew on horseback along narrow trails and rugged terrain, passing through charred remains of the 1987 Silver Fire as they climb to the top of Bald Mountain. This is a special trip lead by Dave Willis, a fierce advocate for protection of this richly biodiverse area. Meet Lou Gold, a resident of the area that shows us flowers and offers up a ceremony for the area. Continue the trip down into the Illinois River Canyon.

Join Oregon Field Guide and two ODFW wildlife biologists as they take a trip on horse through the Wallowa Mountains in the northeast corner of Oregon. ODFW makes this trip annually, tracking a small heard of Big Horn Sheep. OFG is looking for the local black bear which are highly concentrated in this corner of the state. Learn more about the black bear and their habitat. See other local wildlife as well.

Oregon lighthouses have been operating for over 120 years, some still remain active. From Yaquina Head, view the stormy sea looking out through windows. The Umpqua Lighthouse lantern is a specular marvel with over 2 tons of glass and 1,000 prisms. Bandon, Cape Blanco, and Heceta Head lighthouses are all part of a rich history linked to high seas danger and shipwrecks. The Coos Bay Coast Guard now manages the active lighthouses by remote control.

In Scandinavian countries Orienteering draws thousands of fans and competitors, in Oregon the sport is just getting started. Spend a day with the Columbia River Orienteering club as their members introduce you to this fun and challenging sport. The club welcomes everyone to join in the fun. Watch some of the days race participants to see if this is sport you've been waiting for.

Half-hour special report on economic and environmental demands that have taxed the Klamath River basin over the last century. Interviews with miners, ranchers, fishermen, and loggers give us a better understanding of the region's complex problems.

Biathlete Sam Cordell takes us through a winter biathlon course, tracking his speed on cross country skis and mixing it with his ability at target shooting and marksmanship. The sport challenges the fast paced rigor of physical activity with the requirements of calm precise movements in handling a rifle. There is also a summer version of this sport, where running replaces skiing. See how this sport is gaining in popularity here in the states.

The Chinese played a major role during the gold mining days of the 1860's in eastern Oregon. Archeologist Priscilla Wegars, an expert on northwest Chinese history leads participants of the Forest Service's "Passports in Time" archeological program as they unearth an old building site outside of Granite, Oregon. Visit the Kam Wah Chung Museum in John Day and view other fascinating artifacts from that era.
John Day Flowers Photo Essay

The increasing expense of shipping garbage to landfills, and a glut in the recycling market, puts pressure on the waste disposal business. Some resort to dumping garbage illegally in the woods or over cliffs.

A group of physically challenged campers packs into the Three Sisters Wilderness for a four day outing. Riding in on horseback allows the group to enjoy the scenic mountain area that they otherwise would be unable to access.

Underwater microphones, called hydrophones, reveal that underwater earthquakes and other volcanic activity are much more common than scientists once imagined. This activity off the Oregon coast may be affecting the climate.

Border collies were bred in Scotland to help control large herds of sheep. They are still used for work, but more often, in Oregon, raising and training these intelligent dogs is a hobby. Their sheep herding skills are reserved for formal competition.

In a heard of cattle its hard to separate just one cow from the group, and that's where "cutters" come in. Cutters are highly specialized at this one task. It takes years of training, but also natural ability to be a great cutter. We profile Norm and Carmen Bryant of Yamhill Oregon, they operate a breeding and training facility which develops both riders and horses for this thrilling sport.

Fish scientists gather in the southeastern Oregon desert to count the threatened Lahontan cutthroat trout. Grazing cattle have damaged the trout's native streams, but lately, ranchers are changing their ways.

Follow biologists as they conduct their research to protect Oregon's sturgeon. Learn more about this amazing fish, known to grow to lengths of 20 feet and weigh close to 1,500 lbs. Because of the dams on the Snake and Columbia River, their environment has been severely altered. Biologists collect data to set harvest rates and understand more fully how this fish is to survive.

Join paleontologist Dave Taylor and his team as they work to remove a hadrosaur fossil bone embedded in sandstone on the Oregon coast. The hadrosaur or duck-bill was a common herbavore of the Upper Cretaceous Period over 80 million years ago.
In the Columbia Basin of Eastern Oregon, good soil and sunshine are plentiful but water is scarce. Farmers are using sensors and computers to optimize the efficiency of their irrigation systems.
Three outdoor spots accessible to people with disabilities.

Jim Anderson, a man who has devoted his life to Oregon birds of prey, takes us to his secret sites. He shows us raptor nests in central Oregon and bands young birds of prey, including golden eagles, ferruginous hawks, Swainson's hawks and red-tailed hawks.

The majestic peaks of the Wallowa Mountains in Northeast Oregon were mud deposits at the bottom of the ocean about 220 million years ago. The evidence is in the many fossils found in the area, the remains of sea life like corals, sponges, snails and clams.

Wagonmasters were a valuable asset during the Civil War years of this country. Wagons carried gold along the treacherous back country roads from Silver City to McDermitt, OR supporting the financially strapped government. Local rancher, George Wilkinson, drives the OFG crew along these roads. Bob Boyd of the Oregon High Desert Museum tells us more of the story. Mike Hanley, rancher and stagecoach restorer, takes us for a ride in one of his rigs.
The scenery of Oregon is photographer and retired fire fighter Jerry Hunt. He travels the state, sometimes hiking 20 miles with 80 pounds of photographic gear on his back.

Sauvie Island, at the confluence of the Willamette and Columbia rivers, is home to a wildlife area meant to protect migrating birds. But visitors were loving the place to death. New restrictions to protect the area are annoying to some of those visitors.

Clear Lake in the Cascade Mountains was formed when a volcanic eruption dammed the MacKenzie River thousands of years ago. An expert dive team plunges into the cold water in search of a buried forest.

For athletes who spend their summers on mountain bikes, cyclo-cross provides a short but intense burst of bike-riding action. The race follows a rough, muddy cross-country course that requires riders to carry their bikes part of the way.

Rocky Mountain bighorn sheep were wiped out in Oregon, but since the 1970s they've been reintroduced. After some ups and downs, the herd at Lostine in the Wallowas is doing well, and it's time to move some of these sheep into new range.
A high-tech workhorse keeps the Columbia River deep enough for the big ships. The Army Corps of Engineers has to keep dredging the same spots over and over to keep the minimum depth of 40 feet.

Oregon's volcanoes have a history of catastrophic eruptions. One effect of an eruption is a debris flow, which is like a flash flood of ash and debris as thick as wet cement. Since the eruption of Mount St. Helens in 1980, scientists have been studying this phenomenon more closely.

From seals to elk, "The Wildlife Viewing Guide" is a book full of tips on where to find some of the best wildlife viewing in the state. The publishers hope it will also get folks involved in conservation.

Lifting researchers about 200 feet in the air, the Wind River Canopy Crane offers an unprecedented look at the canopy of an old growth forest. This is the only crane in a temperate forest climate in the world.

It's getting cold in Bear Valley, Oregon, 1600 pregnant cows have to be moved to lower ground. Come on along on one of the last big cattle drives still going on in Oregon, led by generations of cowboys.
Amazing equipment gives researchers access to the the old growth Forest Canopy.

The American Indian inhabitants of upper Harney County never developed agricultureŌĆÜ and they didn't need to. The high desert land offers plenty of hunting and gathering of edible plants, if you know where and when to look.
The Oregon Department of Fish and Wildlife is starting to use images of the earth, taking from satellites orbiting in space, to form wildlife management plans.

Seasonally flooding wetlands are crucial for many species. But most of the land set aside for wildlife is in the mountains and forests, not in the Willamette Valley. Private landowners are working with the government to restore parts of their farms back to natural wetlands habitat.
Early settlers in the Willamette Valley were cut off from the east by the Cascade Mountains. In the 1850s they began building roads through the dense forest and across bare lava beds.

Invertebrates play an essential role in the world's various ecosystems. Without invertebrates, the plants and animals that rely on them as pollinators and food supply, would suffer irreparably. Rediscover some amazing invertebrates that until now were thought to be long extinct. Found nowhere else in the world but Oregon, these beautiful bugs are a special find for entomologist Paul Hammond.

Come on along for a hike on Mount Hoods' Timberline Trail with a group from the City of Portland's Senior Outdoor Program. The program hosted six weekly hikes allowing participants to circle Mount Hood.

Over 100 different sites in the mountains of the Pacific Northwest are rigged up with monitoring equipment that sends information about snow and rainfall into a central database. A range of officials uses the information to manage water usage and warn when flood conditions threaten.
Earlier in the century, sentinels were posted at fire lookouts to keep watch for forest fires. Only a few of the tall wooden towers survive, and fans hope to restore and save the historic structures.

The Hanford Nuclear Reservation, where atomic bombs were developed, has remained off-limits to human beings. It's home to the largest undisturbed grassland in the country, an ecological treasure preserving animals and plants.
Witness a unique relationship between humans and hawks as we explore the sport of falconry. Field Guild follows two Harris' hawks and their handlers on a hunt for wild rabbits.

Every year, juvenile salmon and steelhead are shipped downstream to the mouth of the Columbia on special barges. This allows them to avoid deadly dams on their journey to the sea, but it may cause other problems for the endangered fish.

The E.E. Wilson Wildlife Area is criss-crossed with level roads, making it ideal for a hunter who requires wheelchair accessability. Meet one handicapped man who loves to hunt rabbit so much, he's organized hunts for other disabled folks in the area near Corvallis.
This is a very personal look at how three families are dealing with all of the changes in the logging industry.
A leisurely trip through south central Oregon's Klamath Basin as seen through the eyes of our videographers.

Visit the beauty of Oregon's rugged coastline and the offshore islands to find out more about the regions fascinating seabird population. There are over 1400 rocks and islands which make up the Oregon Island's National Wildlife Refuge, critical for seabird nesting and habitat. Almost 1.2 million seabirds nest yearly on the Oregon Coast. Head out to the Oregon Coast Aquarium for a close up view of Tufted Puffins, Oystercatchers, and Murres.
At the turn of the century, downtown Pendleton's streets were lined with honkytonks. Many had brothels upstairs, and some have been preserved, while others remain uninhabited.
Join a herpatologist who leaves no stone unturned in search of western rattlesnakes, Oregon's only dangerously poisonous reptile. Don't try this at home!

There are 110,000 wild elk in Oregon, and about 300 that live on private ranches. According to the Oregon Department of Fish and Wildlife, the domesticated elk pose a threat to the wild population. Elk ranchers disagree.

This remote area in eastern Oregon offers spectacular rock formations and caves that were once home for prehistoric man. Dramatic and colorful volcanic formations abound in this remarkable canyon. Time and erosion have carved out unique features in the rock which give it a honeycomb effect. The area is administered by the Bureau of Land Management. Visit a hundred year old wild horse trap, and explore caves once used by prehistoric populations.
The Mount Hood Snowmobile Club consists of enthusiasts who like to buzz around out in the snow. The club also gets together to give kids battling cancer a day of fun.
Join us for a leisurely tour of the Upper Klamath National Wildlife Refuge. A canoe trail through the refuge's marshes and wetlands provides a perfect opportunity to spot birds and other wildlife.
The Oregon coast is a beautiful place to build a home, but being this close to the power of the sea can be very dangerous.

Once considered a trash fish, Pacific hake has received a lucrative make-over and is now one of Oregon's top commercial catches. It's name was changed to Pacific whiting, and new processing methods allowed new uses for the fish. At the same time, Asia began clamoring for surimi, a multi-purpose processed fish product used to make imitation crab and other foods.

They do more than make honey. Bees are crucial to the cherry, pear and apple orchards of the Hood River Valley. Professional beekeepers in Oregon rent the use of their bees to farmers as far away as California.

Cave climbing is growing as a recreational sport, but those who seek to preserve these geologic wonders worry that this seemingly innocent activity is causing irreparable damage to the cave's surface. The caves in Bend, Oregon are a fragile legacy of a very violent volcanic age. In order to ensure their existence for appreciation by future generations, recreational cave-goers may be required to cooperate with preservationist efforts.
High school students from Monument, in Northeast Oregon, collect water quality data for school credit. Their measurements are used by government agencies, as well.

The harlequin duck's lifestyle is as distinctive as its black and white markings. Spending most of their time along the coast, they head inland to nest. An OSU biologist scours turbulent mountain streams in search of the ducks' nesting area.
In the hot, dry climate of Harney County, many types of lizard thrive. Meet the horned lizard, the leopard lizard, and the collared lizard, which can eat a small mouse.

Visit beautiful Sauvie Island through the eyes of Oregon Field Guide's photographers. Listen to the songs and calls of Sauvie Island's bird life as you look at species including cormorant, great blue heron, crow, and Californa quail, as well as hundreds of Canada geese and sandhill cranes.

Millions of Americans are allergic to yellow jackets. For some, a sting can be fatal. One Corvallis man is harvesting yellow jackets so that pharmaceutical companies can use them to create a vaccine. He's also learned a lot about their complicated task-sharing society.
The Gooseman knows all there is to know about the migrating Canada geese of Sherman County. Follow Russel Belshee as he keeps tabs on the visitors.

Salamanders, frogs, beetles, spiders and other creatures feed, reproduce and die in the surpringly complex world of a small suburban pond. A herpetologist from Portland Community College points out the features of a rough-skinned salamander, which lives part time underwater and is protected from predators by a powerful toxin. Warning: graphic newt sex scene and explicit snail sex talk.

The elk of Elkhorn Ridge spend their summers up in the mountains, but in the winter, they head to lower elevations to feed. As the lowlands filled with ranches, the elk began to eat and destroy haystacks. But by feeding the elk in winter, managers have solved the conflict.

Hidden away high in the central Cascade Mountains is a bizarre land formation known only to a lucky few. Known as Hell Hole, this geologic feature is actually a tension crack at the edge of an ancient lava flow. A pair of forest service geologists lead us on a dangerous, off-trail adventure to explore this massive crack in the ground.

The osprey, a kind of hawk, dives right into the water to catch fish. These large birds were in serious trouble in the 1960s, but now their numbers are increasing steadily. A researcher says two main factors in their recovery are the banning of the pesticide DDT and osprey's ability to adapt to a more urban environment.

North Lake School is a small school near Christmas Valley that draws students from a vast rural district that was the ancestral home of the Paiute Indians. Members of the tribe deliver a formal blessing to the school and share their legends with students who are mostly of European descent.

Camas, lupine, monkeyflower and fiddleneck are among the more than 800 wildflowers native to the Columbia River Gorge. Russ Jolley and Nancy Russell give us a tour and explain why the gorge is such a good place for biological diversity.

In the 16th century, sailors landed on the Oregon coast near Manzanita. They left behind stones carved with mysterious words and symbols. Legend has it that the stones are keys to finding buried treasure on Neahkahnie Mountain.

The USDA's Animal Damage Control Program eliminates animals that threaten livestock and cause other kinds of mischief. They kill hundreds of thousands of wild animals a year in Oregon, including about 100 bears and 7000 to 8000 coyotes.
Discover the joy and hard work it takes to be a first class rower. Willamette river Oregon

Tree squirrels some of our most familiar wild animals, but also some of the least studied. The play an important role in the ecosystem, helping to distribute the seeds of plants and trees and providing a food source for predators. Non-native species such as gray squirrels and fox squirrels thrive in many urban areas, displacing native species like the western gray squirrel.
Geologists are now convinced ancient, monstrous earthquakes and tsunami waves have battered the Oregon coast again and again. Given its violent past, the big question is, ŌĆ£Can it happen again?ŌĆØ
Run with the dogs as they track bear in the Oregon Blue Mountains. ItŌĆÖs all part of a science project to monitor population trends and learn more about where the bears go and what they eat.

Thanks to dedicated volunteers, children at Camp Agape do all the fun things that kids at summer camp do, even though they have cancer. The camp provides the support needed for these kids, their parents and their siblings to swim, play and get away from it all.
High in the Steens Mountain Canyon, a violent battle for survival is underway, as fierce carnivores stalk unwary prey.

Oregon's system of state parks has long been one of the finest and most used in the nation. But now it's in deep trouble with a quarter of the parks schedules to close. This special report explores the state park crisis and looks at possible solutions.
Endurance racing is a demanding sport that tests the stamina of horse and rider, who must complete a 100-mile course in a single day. The challenge is to pace yourself and your horse.
Jet-powered one- two- and three-person watercraft are increasingly popular, but not everyone loves them. They make a lot of noise and cause a disproportionate number of accidents on the river.
Privately owned Stout Mountain, east of Salem, has remained virtually untouched by human actions. In the heavily-settled Willamette Valley, it's a rare glimpse at the region's natural diversity.
The Barlow Road, once the primary route over the Cascade Mountains to the Willamette Valley, was notorious for causing death, heartbreak and struggle for Oregon pioneers.

The bullfrog is thriving in Oregon. But are they responsible for several native species disappearing? Managers at the Finley Wildlife Refuge work to remove adult bullfrogs from the lake in an effort to control their populations. Researchers are finding evidence that these aggressive predators have had a significant impact on native species such as the western pond turtle and the Oregon chub.
Not even a drizzling Oregon day can distract from the gorgeous colors of this massive tulip field in Woodburn. In full blossom, this sight holds beauty for everyone to enjoy.
Oregon is home to a number of top wildlife artists, as well as many less known. Field Guide talks to artists about how they approach nature and capture images from the wild.
The Ponderosa Guest Ranch south of John Day is a working cattle ranch. It's also a "dude ranch" where folks from the city pay to ride horses and round up the cattle.
A difficult training exercise on the White Mountain Glacier on the slopes of Mount Hood is an audition for climbers hoping to join Portland Mountain Rescue.

In the 1980s, a sewer project on the grounds of Woodburn High School revealed an ancient peat bog. The bog preserved the bones of Ice Age creatures like giant sloths, elephants and bison. Field Guide returns for a new dig in the bog.

When the city of Portland starts work on a light rail line to Beaverton and Hillsboro, engineers have to confront the West Hills. They dig and blast their way through the layers of the area's ancient past of geologic upheaval.

Migratory songbirds are not only melodic and beautiful they also can tell us a lot about the environment. We join two bird surveys which track the health of Oregon's songbird population. First we accompany volunteers from the American Breeding Bird Survey, as they track changes along an annual survey route. Later we stop in on members of the Natural Heritage Program as they band birds and note vital statistics on Portland's Sauvie Island.

Take a look at some of the Willamette Valley's prairie lands. The Nature Conservancy of Oregon and the State are taking inventory of the valley's native plants and wildlife species. See how the prairie has changed due to lack of fire. The oak savanna, one of the rarest and quickly diminishing of habitats is being crowded out by plants normally held at bay by fires. See how lack of this habitat impacts the survival of wildlife.

The discovery of the oldest skeleton in the Pacific Northwest has uncovered a murder mystery 90 centuries old. It's also created new controversy. The analysis of Kennewick Man by scientists is opposed by his American Indian descendants.

Extreme weather in February of 1996 caused flooding through the Cascade Mountain range. The cleanup costs millions and it will be decades before the forest recovers. We'll show how humans contributed to the damage, and what they're doing to reverse it.

Waldo's unique water quality is so pure the visibility is over 120 feet deep. Volunteer scientists are worried, however, that the quality may be decreasing. Fish stocking and other human interactions with the lake, have greatly altered the natural biological communities of the lake. Waldo-lovers urge visitors to respect the purity of the lake and hope that government dollars will help to protect it.

There are some serious problems with the Willamette River's water quality. The river has been cleaned up considerably since the 1960s, when it was basically an open sewer, but more subtle problems persist.
A report on the controversy surrounding land management practices and their impact on endangered fish in Klamath Lake in Central oregon

Started in 1993 the Oregon Fish and Wildlife department has been sponsoring the Outdoor Women program. Since than it's popularity has only grown. The program teaches woman a variety of outdoor skills including fishing and hunting. Survival skills are also on the agenda as well as use of firearms.

Straw bale construction provides an energy efficient resource for buildings. Visit with a Bend family in the process of building their own straw bale house. Hear from their builder Tod Wooldridge and see some of his work. Architect Linda Barnes talks of the benefits of straw bale construction and shows us her latest project. See a number of examples of this technology.

Join host Steve Amen for a fun-filled half hour as her answers questions kids K-12 have about Oregon. Find out what the biggest animal in Oregon is or just how the state got its name. Many of the answers will surprise you.
Witness the violent battle to keep the slopes safe for skiers as resort owners fight the threat of an avalanche. Mt Hood and Mt Bachelor Oregon
Tired of riding your bike alone, weŌĆÖll show you some group bike rides that appeal to everyone. Portland Oregon
South central OregonŌĆÖs lush Sycan Marsh is a showcase restoration project thanks to some very unconventional methods.
ItŌĆÖs a wild look at a fading tradition as we return to the Oregon coast for a report on dory fisherman. Pacific City Oregon
A look at why Coho salmon populations are on the decline in OregonŌĆÖs coastal rivers.┬Ā
Watch mother nature at work as we see Smith Rock State Park bounce back after a devastating fire. Smith Rock State Park Oregon
South Central Oregon
You'll be amazed at some of the hidden wonders in the coast range as we hit the trails in search of little known waterfalls / scenery
A report on why some fires are being allowed to burn in Oregonšs wilderness areas.
You'll meet an amazing woman who spent her life learning how to observe animals and recognize the signs they leave behind. Central oregon
The number of pronghorn antelope is declining in most Oregon because something is killing most fawns within days of their birth. OFG goes looking for answers.
