Snowboarding continues to grow in popularity, but as the snowboarders get more experience, the desire to push the envelope is leading many of them into dangerous situations. Central Oregon
The sport of mushing a sled dog team is catching on in Oregon and we will follow a couple of women who find out just what it takes to handle a team.
Crater Lake is Oregon's only national park and a major tourist attraction. But there's concern the area may be in trouble so scientists are investigating ways to preserve its fragile beauty
We take to the air to join hang gliders sailing high above the Oregon coastline.
It's a leisurely float trip down the John Day River as we see spectacular scenery and learn a lot about local history of Native Americans along the way.
Your chance to see this spectacular travel destination as few have. Crater lake National Park.
Safety tips to protect you when hiking.
There are a number of people who believe that stocking our rivers and streams with hatchery fish is the answer to our depleted fish populations, but the latest research doesnŌĆÖt back this up
These lakes, located in North Portland, make up the largest urban wetland in the United States.
Take a look at the beauty of Central Oregon's Newberry Crater and learn a lot more about how it was formed.
It has been called a "biological emergency." Noxious weeds are taking over huge areas of Oregon land- killing native plants and destroying the land.
This is your chance to step back into time and head to the beautiful Blue Mountains for a ride on a wagon train.┬Ā

This half-hour special graphically examines the deadly potential of Mt Rainier, considered the most dangerous Cascade volcano of them all. Emergency teams are making plans for dealing with catastrophic mud flows that could bury towns miles away. And as the experts show us, this danger exists even if there isn't an eruption.
We join a group of volunteers near Mt. Hood oregon to find out a lot more about the migration patterns of these elusive birds of prey.
Hemp is one of the world's oldest sources of fiber for clothing and paper. Some variieties are illegal to grow in Oregon, but that may be changing.
We head to the Oregon coast for a look at a relatively new sport that is getting more popular in Oregon.
Under the cloak of darkness researchers study Western Oregon bats to find out what they need to survive in a changing environment.
Thousands of Oregonians love this hobby. OFG looks at what makes it so appealing and why it is so controversial.
Researchers use the latest technology to get a better understanding of the threats to salmon and bull trout in the John Day River. Oregon
Find out what are the best ways to attract birds as we show you which bird feeders and what kinds of feed work best.
Dedicated volunteers take special needs and terminally ill kids out for a day of fishing. For many, it's their first chance to forget their troubles and enjoy the outdoors.
This area, east of Salem oregon in the foothills of the Cascades, is the largest in-tact old growth forest in the Northwest. Find out what's being done to protect the area.
Find out how a local company is using leaves to help keep our streams and waterways clean.┬Ā

column4: 09.13 Bald Eagles of the Lower Columbia
column5: A look at why the numbers of eagles, hawks and owls that winter here are on the decline.┬Ā
The Hobie Cats are one of the best selling sailboats in the world - we'll show you why.
A fascinating look back at the 1870's war between the US Army and the Modoc tribe over the Klamath Basin oregon

Spectacular floods radically changed the landscape of Oregon and Washington some 17,000 years ago when Ice Age glacial dams failed near Missoula, Montana. These floods unleashed an inland sea and geologists believe they are the greatest floods ever known to humankind. Evidence is most easily seen from the air and includes ripple marks and dry falls. The floods dropped huge boulders and deposited mountains of topsoil in the Willamette valley.
The Mount Hood ski areas continue to lead the nation in promoting skiing for people with disabilities. Explore the new technology and meet the people opening our ski slopes to everyone.

In storm or sunshine this crowd makes for the steeps. Sno-cats allow some of the world's best skiers to conquer even the most remote parts of Mt. Bailey. No chairlifts, groomed slopes, nor gentle ski runs, Mt. Bailey is a cloud-shrouded mystery to most. Potential avalanches and thick tree cover makes this area too dangerous for beginners, but for these extreme skiers this area offers a much-sought-after feeling of solitude and discovery.

Jeeps, ATVs and dirtbikes are allowed in designated areas of the Tillamook State Forest. It's one of the only places near the Portland Metro area where these sports are allowed, and care is taken to minimize damage to soil and water.

Scientists find new ways to monitor the Dungeness crabs in Coos Bay. They're trying to understand why crabs are abundant some years and scarce in others. They hope to learn how to keep the crab population, and the industry that relies on it, healthy.

Rodeo is a rough way for a cowboy to make a living, but cowboys aren't the only athletes competing here. Half the points from a ride come from how the animal performs. Go behind the scenes to see how the animals live when they're not in the ring.

The field guide crew travels to Netarts Bay on the Oregon coast, and joins some of the many individuals helping to restore Oregon's endangered native oyster population. Commercial oysterman, State biologists, and water quality managers are all playing a vital role in re-introducing one of Oregon's most threatened species.

Oregon is known for its gray skies and rainfall, and seems a unlikely place for solar energy. Yet Oregon is one of the leaders in photo voltaic energy production. We take a look a some of the individuals and architects who are incorporating solar energy in to their everyday life.

They look like big mud puddles, but vernal pools - ponds that appear in spring and dry up in summer - are unique ecosystems that are just starting to be understood. In Oregon, they are home to the federally-protected fairy shrimp.

Some folks call it "a rat with wings." The common pigeon is native to the cliffs of Asia and Africa, but they have adapted well to city life and are found in metropolitan areas around the world. The birds cause significant damage to buildings, bridges, and other structures. Find out a lot more about these birds and meet the folks who either love them or hate them.

This beautiful wetland, located at the base of Southern Oregon's Hart Mountain, has recently been restored - creating habitat for a large variety of wildlife. Biologist Walt DeVores guides you through the Warner Wetlands. The Warner Wetlands provide habitat to a variety birds such as Ruddy Ducks, Avocet's, Long Billed curloos and Black-crowned Night-Heron. There are also predators like the Burrowing Owl, and Northern Harrier.

Wood ducks are water birds but they nest in hollow trees, or, sometimes, in nesting boxes built by friendly humans. A student project counts the eggs in an area of western Oregon and reports to the Fish and Wildlife Service.
The number of honeybees who live in the wild in Oregon has been diminished by 98 percent by parasitic mites. When the Asian broodmites move in, they destroy entire colonies at a time.
Razor clams are plentiful on the Oregon Coast, along with several other species. Digging for clams takes a little skill, but for some it's a family tradition. For others, it's a source of income.
High up on the ridges of Oregon's Wallowa Mountains, biologists find a healthy herd of Rocky Mountain goats. There may only be a few hundred in the state, but they're well protected and doing fine.

When a rancher agreed to keep his cattle out of Bear Creek for part of the year, he knew it would help the creek, but he didn't realize how much it would help his ranching business. Field Guide traces recovery of the creek, near Prineville, and asks why more ranchers haven't done the same.
A small island in the Columbia estuary is home to the largest nesting colony of Caspian terns in the world. That spells disaster to millions of juvenile salmon trying to migrate out to sea.
This is a look at a different type of wetland restoration ŌĆō one that is accomplished by digging a new shallow stream in a relatively short period of time if all the conditions are right.┬Ā

Archeologists are finding intriguing evidence of civilization in South Central Oregon's high desert country. People lived and thrived near Fort Rock more than 10,000 years ago. But looting is having a dramatic effect on archaeologists attempting to learn more about the past.
As hand-held global positioning system (GPS) devices become available, hikers take them out for test run on Mount Hood.
The Western bluebird was once a common songbird in the Willamette Valley, but development has all but wiped them out. We'll show you what's being done to bring them back.

There may be as few as 700 lynx in the western United States but the animals are extremely hard to count. They hunt at night and prefer deep snow. Near Mount Adams, a team of researchers combine simple lures and high tech DNA analysis to count the lynx population.
Light and fast, outrigger canoes allowed ancient Polynesians to cross vast stretches of open ocean. Outrigger racing is becoming increasingly popular on Oregon rivers.

Jim Henderson learned a photographic technique called cross polarization when he was a medical photographer. Now he's using his skill to document and enhance American Indian rock art that is faded, damaged, and in danger of disappearing completely.

Learn more about these elusive forest residents. It used to be Martens only lived in high altitude old growth, but now they're turning up in lodgepole pine country. Join researchers as they trap and collect vital information regarding Oregon's Marten population. Radio collars and motion sensor video are playing a key role in understanding the Martens behavior.

A world-class showcase garden called the Oregon Garden is scheduled to open in the year 2000. Field Guide follows preparations for the opening of the 240-acre world class botanical garden meant to be a showcase for Oregon's booming nursery industry.

Among the earliest settlements of Europeans in Oregon, the town of Champoeg thrived in the 1840s and 1850s. Archeologists are finding traces of the city, which was washed away by a flood, and using a radar device to locate city streets underneath a field of rye grass.
Geologists say the east side of Portland was once the west coast of the North American continent. WeŌĆÖll show you why as we visit a prime clue to the ancient history of the Willamette River.

California Sea Lions are invading Oregon's bays and estuaries. There is mounting concern the growing sea lion population is putting the survival of some endangered salmon and steelhead at risk. Oregon Fish and Wildlife is studying the Sea Lion population in an effort to better understand if certain individual's are more problematic.

Birdwatchers have long known that wastewater treatment plants can be prime sites for viewing migrating and wintering waterfowl. The nutrient-rich waters of the treatment ponds provide ideal habitat for dozens of species. At a facility near Nahalem, Bill Tice shows us what attracts both birds and birders to these areas.
We head 30 miles off the Oregon coast, with the help of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, for a look at the undersea volcano and what scientists are just now learning about it.

Scientists track osprey across thousands of miles of winter migration using cutting edge satellite tracking. They're surprised to find birds that mate for life actually spend half a year far apart, then reunite during breeding season.

In 50 years of operation, the Salvage Chief has rescued almost every major vessel in trouble on the west coast. Including the Exxon Valdez, making it probably the most accomplished salvage vessel in the world. Filmed weeks before the latest grounding near Coos Bay.
Find out how weed-eating insects are becoming an effective means of controlling unwanted, invasive plants and discuss and dangers of introducing non-native insects to our state of Oregon
Visit some of Portland's historic sites and see how the Portland Heights area has changed as we go hiking with the Mazamas during one of their training walks.

Meet Oregon's own 85-year-old "horse whisperer". John Sharp has been "gentling" wild horses for over 60 years. We'll see how he does it and learn a little bit about the horse sense he's picked up over the years. He uses a bamboo pole, a rope, and a calm approach to transform wild horses into loyal saddle horses.

Coast Guard trainees come from all over the country to practice rescue missions in some of the most treacherous water in the world: Oregon's North Coast surf during a winter storm. Students in the Coast Guard's elite rescue swimmer program face the churning whitewater and the steep rocky cliffs.

A group of 20 die-hard bird watchers head out to sea during the stormy end of the winter season in search of pelagic birds. These are birds who spend almost their whole life at sea, such as albatross, shearwaters and petrals.

Western grebes, as well as five other types of grebe, congregate in the waters of Upper Klamath Lake. These waterfowl have a unique courtship ritual in which they appear to dance, as couples, across the surface of the lake.

Humans lived in the caves of the remote Owyhee River Valley as long as 12,000 years ago. Artifacts and petroglyphs offer clues to this little-studied people, but money for research is scarce and looting is damaging the evidence.

For some backpackers with back problems, goats are the solution for long treks. They're extremely mobile, and an adult male goat can carry 50 to 60 pounds. But it's a serious commitment keeping goats as pets.

For a small number of hobbyists, flying is as easy as hooking a parachute up to a motor and a three-wheeled buggy. Powered parachute pilots soar over central Oregon, but they have to get home before the wind picks up.

Spiders are fierce but tiny predators that invade our homes, often undetected. Some types of spiders have adapted over thousands of years to live alongside humans, gaining information about their surroundings mainly by sensing vibrations.

Once extinct in Oregon, the Columbian sharp-tailed grouse is making a comeback in Wallowa county. Once vast numbers nested in the grass, but they disappeared completely until members of the small population in Idaho were reintroduced into Oregon.

Gateway to Devil's Canyon and the Snake river in northeastern Oregon, Imnaha is a small, isolated community, home to friendly, independent people as well as the occasional cougar. With a population of about two hundred, this tiny community attracts few newcomers due to its isolation, rugged terrain and intense summer heat. Steeped in history, this land was once home to the Nez Perce Indians and then later, Euro-American settlers.

The red wiggler worm is making quite a name for itself in the waste management business. Dan Holcombe of Portland uses the worms to process 5000 pounds a day of rejected food and produce, turning it into valuable fertilizer.

The Bonneville Slide of 400 years ago completely blocked the Columbia River, creating the mythical Bridge of the Gods. Eventually, it collapsed in a massive flood. Today, a highway bridge in a narrow part of the river marks the spot.

Follow amateur odonatist Steve Valley as he studies and catalogs the amazing variety of dragonflies and damselflies in Oregon. Scientific knowledge of this colorful and fascinating insect species is limited and Steve and other volunteers are working to learn more about the dragonfly's lifecycle from nymph to adult, their mating habits and how habitat destruction and harvesting for fish bait is affecting their populations.
And would you believe that a lake in central Oregon is one of the best places in the U.S. to catch Atlantic salmon?

For a few weeks each fall, Chapman elementary school in NW Portland is home to the largest concentration of Vaux swifts in North America. The thousands of swifts who come to roost in the school's chimney provide a bird-watching spectacle that draws crowds from around the city. Volunteers from Portland Audubon Society are on hand to answer the public's questions.
The variety of snakes that are native to the Willamette Valley is incredible. Field Guide takes a look at these cold blooded critters.

As expert rodent catchers, barn owls are an invaluable resource for farmers. Meet Scott, a LaGrande man who spends a lot of his time working to improve life for barn owls. Putting up over 60 nesting boxes in barns across his community, Scott has inspired a movement to protect and preserve the barn owl population in the Grande Ronde Valley. Learn about these beautiful birds and their need for good places to nest.
Oregon is the Easter Lily capital of the world. Find out why from a man who has been in the business for 25 years. Willamette Valley Oregon

SNewberry Crater is a caldera, the product of a powerful volcanic explosion. Burried under volcanic ash, ancient artifact suggest that humans lived comfortably here for thousands of years... until the massive explosion that devastated the area. Numerous eruptions of lava and ash made the area inhabitable for centuries by humans. Visit the Newberry Crater today and witness the alpine paradise it has become - until the volcano explodes again.
To get a good tree, it takes more than just putting a seed in the ground. Commercial nurseries use clones, rather than seeds, to cultivate consistent, top-quality trees, and it's a lot of work.

Location Hidden
Underwater divers get a glimpse of the fragile remains of an ancient canoe resting at the bottom of a Cascade lake. Sediment has hidden the canoe, perhaps for centuries. It's made from a Ponderosa pine and was probably used by native people to harvest water lily roots.

The Tualatin Riverkeepers are a group of dedicated volunteers who cherish the beauty and history of the Tualatin River. The groups' ultimate goal is to get others to help take care of this special river, but they know in order to do so they will have to get people to appreciate it first. As the river's water quality continues to improve and wildlife begins to flourish again, the Riverkeepers are confident that their mission will be a success.

Jet pilots need constant training to maintain their edge in the cockpit. Oregon's more remote areas offer the pilots of the Air National Guard a good place to practice their more dangerous moves. Now there is growing concern about the impact on wildlife and the environment from more than 4500 training flights per year.
From April through November, Vista Balloon Adventures in Newberg launches hot air balloons over scenic Yamhill County. Visitors enjoy a ride that is slow and quiet, but exciting nonetheless.

When Crook Point on the southern coast became available for sale, developers were interested. But thanks to some quick work from the Nature Conservancy, it will remain a pristine, rare refuge for a large array of wildlife, with human access strictly limited.
The poplar, once taken for granted, has a lot of uses and may actually become an important factor in our state's economy.

Beryl Grilley is 90 years old and still herding cattle on horseback. She and a team of other grandmas volunteer to help their neighbors move cattle. Beryl is an inductee into the Pendleton Round Up Hall of Fame.

Several golf courses around the state are changing the way they operate in an effort to improve wildlife habitat. Several have become Audubon Cooperative Sanctuaries, working to make the courses hospitable to birds.

Oregon Department of Transportation divers take some pretty big risks to make sure the bridges we drive on are safe. After a flood, divers must brave cold water, swift currents and debris to inspect the underwater footings of bridges.

This short film by Nick Fisher focuses on southern Oregon's Cape Arago. Nick eloquently captures Cape Arago's many moods. Beautiful sunsets and rolling clouds are vividly captured, as well as some stunning shots of the beach at high and low tide.

All along the Oregon Coast, powerful waves, human action, and even earthquakes cause the coastline to shift. Scientists are concerned about evidence that sand is disappearing from our coastline, and they warn about building too close to the edge.

An update on some amazing archeological discoveries that are turning up in digs around the town, including the ice age fossils of a bird with a wingspan of 14 feet - the largest ever found in the Northwest.

At 300 feet, Munson Creek Falls, near Tillamook, is the highest waterfall in the Coast Range. When a timber company put the land around the creek up for sale, an alliance including the River Conservancy and the Paul Allen Forest Protection Fund bought the land and turned it over to the state.

Alan Kapuler is a gardener and seed saver who is preserving heirloom varieties of vegetables and flowers and well as breeding new varieties on his organic farm in Corvalis, Oregon. Seed savers are individuals working to maintain genetic diversity in our food, flowers and plants by saving, trading and sharing seeds.
A group of experienced kayakers discover true whitewater adventure, excitement and camaraderie on the White Salmon River in SW Washington.

Most of us have never heard of the boomer, or mountain beaver, but it is a major threat to the forest services industry. A close cousin of the more common beaver, boomers are a small burrowing rodent and also one of the oldest. Numbering in the hundreds of thousands they range along the coastal mountains throughout much of the pacific NW. Take a look at what is being done to mitigate the damage they cause to reseeded forest.

Elizabeth Thomas has spent years documenting the migratory return of these beautiful little birds. Since 1996, fourteen year old Elizabeth has been collecting reports of first sightings of the rufous hummingbird as it returns to Oregon. Spend time with her as she talks about the birds and their habitat.
Microscopic images bring the amazing but violent world of tiny insects into focus. These miniature creatures play a vital role in the environment.

Laura and Warren Halsey, owners of Raindance Ranch created a diverse native habitat on this 250 acre farm. The property had been heavily farmed for over a century. Through their own hard work, the Halseys with the support of government agencies and others were able to blend the elements of commercial farming with the presence of oak savannas, forests and wet lands. See the results of their efforts in the return of native plants and wildlife.

They may look like they're clowning around, but the rodeo clown's job is the riskiest job in rodeo. They spend more time putting on protective gear than they do painting their faces, because there's nothing between a clown and a bucking bull, and cowboys depend on them for their lives.

We visit Steens Mountain in eastern Oregon, an isolated, rugged landscape that attracts only the truly adventurous. Carved out by ancient glaciers, Steens rises 10,000 ft. above sea level, with steep canyon walls covered in 12ft of snow. Visitors on snowmobiles and x-country skis can expect to find beauty and solitude here, especially in winter, with thousands of acres of quiet, snow covered hills to explore.

This half-hour special looks at the changes in Portland's landscape over the last 150 years and how they have had fatal implications to local fish. WeŌĆÖll also show you whatŌĆÖs being done today to try and bring the fish back.

Yurt, tent, or trailer? Which suits a family of four the best on a multiple-night camping trip? Join host Steve Amen and his family on a camping adventure where they try out each different accommodation for a few nights in an effort to find the ultimate, luxurious-yet-affordable camping experience.

Join the Fairchild family of Philomath, OR and their dog as they explore the life and habitat of the Oregon rain beetle. They visit with entomologist Rick Wescott who gives them an in-depth look at this curious resident of Oregon.

Wildlife experts track the health of peregrine falcons from the undergirders of Portland's Fremont Bridge to the foothills of the Oregon Cascades. The falcons are returning from the brink of extinction and raising their young across the state.

Now you can hit the road and travel along an unpaved roadway through some of the most scenic and seldom visited parts of Oregon because they require four-wheel drive and a knowledge that such places even exist. Long forgotten wagon tracks meander through Oregon's back country and are the source for some 750 miles of road which make up the Back Country Discovery Route - a one of a kind Oregon experience.

Oregon artist Heather Fortner shows us the ancient Japanese art of fish printing using some familiar northwest fish. Gyotaku is a unique art form which as been practiced for hundreds years in Japan and is making its presence felt within Oregon's growing arts community.
See the incredible work that goes on behind the scenes of the aquariumŌĆÖs ŌĆ£Passage of the DeepŌĆØ exhibit to make sure hundreds of species thrive in this unnatural world. Newport Oregon

Oregon silverspot butterflies rely on dwindling amounts of coastal grassland, and the butterflies' numbers are plummeting. A cooperative effort is launched to try to save the butterflies before it's too late.

They live all around us, but what do we really know about the clever crow? One of the smartest birds in the world, the crow is a master at solving environmental puzzles. Highly socialized the crow thrives in urban centers and has adapted to living within close proximity to humans. Join a team of researchers as they trap and band both crows and its much larger cousin the Raven.
Spend a weekend on the river with Lenox Dick, also known as the "dean of the Deschutes fly fishermen", and his buddy Tom McAllister as they reminisce about old times and share good fishing tips.

A first-hand look at the risks of working on an ocean-going vessel as we follow scientists studying Dungeness Crab larvae. There's nothing boring about this research project, high seas are a constant challenge for the tiny ship and crew. Dungeness Crab are an important part of Oregon's coastal economy, yet very little is known about their life cycle. With extensive data collection the crew of the McArthur hopes to shed light on the mystery.

Using advanced mapping technology, scientists are able to study the varied contours of the bottom of Crater Lake. Until now, the depths of the lake, which was formed by a massive volcanic explosion, were a mystery. Now computer technology can process acoustic signals bounced off the bottom.

A couple of years ago, officials decided to adopt the controversial practice of allowing some fires to burn in selected Oregon wilderness areas in the hope of preventing major forest fires. In this report we see how the program is working.

Vulnerable to the dangers of the urban environment, many injured birds end up at the Audubon Society Wildlife Care Center. When large birds of prey are injured and taken to the Wildlife Care Center for help, their recovery depends upon the of time and care of dedicated people.
ItŌĆÖs a race against time as a woman works to document the history behind the tree carvings by long gone Basque sheep herders in Harney County.

NASA scientists explore the deep blue waters of Oregon's Crater Lake as a trial run for a future trip to one of Jupiter's moons, Europa. Europa appears to have a deep, cold sea that NASA plans to explore one day on an unmanned mission.

Smith Rock near Madras Oregon has become the United Nations of Sport Climbing, because it attracts so many climbers from across the globe. Oregon native Alan Watts has done more than anyone to call attention to Smith Rock. He has helped develop the sport and mapped out most of the climbs which make Smith Rock a climbers paradise.

Evidence of the life and activities of Cy Bingham, one of Oregon's earliest forest rangers, can still be found today in the trees where he carved his name. At the turn of the last century Cy Bingham was one of only a handful of federal forest rangers within the Oregon Cascades. Mark Dunaway is trying to catalog the many trees and trails where Cy left his mark, but it is a race against time as the forest has begun to cover Cy's tracks.

Pat Collins is the last of the muskrat trappers. He makes his living trapping non-native muskrats in the Klamath Basin Wildlife Refuge. The muskrat tunnels damage the system of levees and dykes that was built to create the marshlands. Although he doesn't make much money from the pelts, Collins' trapping helps control the population of muskrats in the refuge and keeps the wetlands intact for the birds.

The West Coast Game Park, located on 21 acres near Bandon, is a walk-through safari park as well as a giant petting zoo. Visitors are nuzzled by llamas and deer, and they get close to monkeys, lions, tigers and more. The park also breeds large cats.
Different species of aquatic insects survive the hostile environment of inland freshwater streams. Some spend part of their life cycle underwater and some of it in the air.

This hardy species has been around for thousands of years and even survived the volcanic eruption that created Crater Lake. But now it's near extinction as we look at the epic struggle now going on to reestablish this ancient species.

A look at Warner Canyon Ski Area, outside of Lakeview, a small town operation run by locals and staffed by volunteers. Don Lindsey is a volunteer and longtime skier who's four sons also give their time to this community treasure, fixing the aging ski lift and grooming the slopes. The small ski area started as a lone trail created by clear cutting and was slowly expanded into what is now a thriving local attraction.

Cave Diving is the most dangerous type of diving there is. Meet a team of researchers who take the risk to explore parts of an Oregon cave that have never been seen before. The cave is a restricted site located in the lava lands near the Idaho border and is believed to be the only divable freshwater cave in the Pacific Northwest. The divers use kayaks, flashlights, and scuba gear to explore the thermally heated lava cave.

The Oregon Dunes Mail Run is one of the oldest and least-known of dog sled musher runs - maybe because it's not in the snow. The race covers 70 miles up and down steep dunes, forest paths and watery bogs from Reedsport to Florence. In bygone times, this wild corridor was the only way to travel between coastal towns.

Commercial trawl nets scoop up everything in their path, both commercially valuable fish and many others that they aren't legally allowed to keep. These fish are simply thrown back into the water. Now scientists at the Hatfield Marine Science Center in Newport are studying how many of these thrown back fish can survive.
Join us for a two day climb up the mountain renowned for unpredictable and dangerous weather with a posse of adventurous snowboarders. The goal is a twenty minute ride back home.
An investigation into boulder-sized rocks from as far away as Canada that litter the Willamette Valley.

Introduced to the area as an ornamental garden plant, English Ivy is spreading quickly in Portland's Forest Park, choking out other native plant life. The No Ivy League is a group of people who have declared war on the so-called "green plague," volunteering their time for the hard work of removal and restoration.

A unique look at the Tillamook Railroad, an economic lifeline shuttling goods over the sometimes treacherous Oregon Coast Range. Engineer, Larry Miller, guides the three engine train up the steep, Salmonberry River Canyon and across spindly, aging bridges exposed to unpredictable landslides carrying car-sized boulders.

In between glassy, calm waters there is the occasional white water on the Grande Ronde, but this rafting trip is not about navigating through violent rapids or roughing it overnight on baked beans and hot dogs... Floating, fishing, feasting and fun seem to be the theme of this tour guided trip down the scenic river. Join the OFG crew for a leisurely float down one of Oregon's hidden treasures.

Meet a group of pioneers who have taken windsports to a whole new level. With a modified kite, a board, and a strong wind, these thrillseekers race across the water and fly up to 100 feet into the air over the Columbia River near Hood River.
There is renewed interest in using wind power to replace hydropower as more and more demands are being placed on river water.┬Ā

In Roseburg, Oregon, biologist Dwain Jackson pursues the fawns of a newly recovered species of deer, the Columbia White Tail. By radio tagging and monitoring the progress of the fawns in the wild, biologists can definitively say that the population is making a comeback. Elk in the surrounding area, however, are competing with the struggling species of deer. Elk can be captured and temporarily relocated, but the solution is hardly a permanent one.

The Grizzly Mountain Long Rifles formed in the early 1970's and the group has grown to 119 families. The Long Rifles are different from other Mountain Man or Rendezvous organizations in that they meet once a month for club shoots and involve all family members. However, they do have two Rendezvous a year. One is their Spring Shoot in May and the other big gathering occurs each February just east of Bend.
After years of hard work, the dream of a trail that will span all of Oregon's coast, over 400 miles in length, is becoming a reality. The Oregon Coast Trail is nearly complete.

When migrating seabirds stop along the Oregon coast to nest and raise their young, it's a prime opportunity for scientists to count them. See some of Oregon's more colorful visitors, including cormorants and common murres, as Field Guide follows scientists who take photos of the birds from high in a helicopter.

Hydroelectric dams are a dangerous obstacle for young salmon on their way downriver. New technology allows scientists to send a computerized probe called a sensor fish through the turbulent route followed by salmon through the dam. The hope is to learn ways to minimize fish mortality.

The Zumwalt Prairie Preserve is the largest purchase made by the Nature Conservancy in Oregon. This rolling prairie is a fragile ecosystem home to fifteen species of raptors, like the Red Tail Hawk and Short Eared Owl. Ecologists like Dennis Sheehy study the effects of a hundred years of homesteading and cattle grazing to determine how best to manage these delicate grasslands.

There is more than one way to harvest trees. Companies who avoid clear-cutting and follow other sustainable practices can be certified by the Forest Stewardship Council. Field Guide takes a look at a certified forest.

Join us as we explore an ancient mystery of hand and foot prints embedded in a lava flow that's at least 8,000 years old. The prints are now under the water of a lake near Mount Adams. Were they carved, or made by human hands and feet?

The Oregon Museum of Science and Industry (OMSI) runs a camp for kids at the Oregon coast near Tillamook. The kids learn about the world around them with the help of one especially enthusiastic instructor at Camp MacGruder.

Landscape and garden nurseries are thriving in Oregon. But this industry is responsible for a huge waste problem because it uses so many plastic tubs and pots. Salem-based Agri-Plas Recycling is tackling the problem head-on.

Researchers begin a captive breeding program for pygmy rabbits at the Oregon Zoo. In the field, they puzzle over the sudden, catastrophic decline of the rabbit population, which has all but disappeared from its native sagebrush habitat in Oregon and Washington.

The voracious tussock moth has wiped out thousands of acres of Douglas fir in Oregon. This devastation is really part of the natural cycle of the forest, as long as it doesn't get out of hand. Scientists have figured out how to control the moth's populations so that the infestations become beneficial.

Life-threatening spinal deformities are appearing in large numbers of juvenile fish in a stretch of the Willamette River called the Newberg Pool. This portion of the river stretches from Newberg to Oregon City. Scientists are trying to unravel the mystery by studying both the fish and contaminants in the river.

The Burns Paiute Tribe has re-acquired former territory in Logan Valley. The tribe was able to purchase many thousands of acres of their former land. We look at the efforts of the tribe to regain not only lands, but tradition.

The numbers of salmon returning to the upper Tualatin River have dwindled. One consequence is a lack of dead salmon to nourish the river and its banks. Boy Scout volunteers learn how tossing away a truckload of dead fishery salmon is actually good for the environment.

The rare Aleutian Geese known as Semidi birds winter off the Oregon Coast near Pacific City. Only 126 birds make up the Semidi birds a sub-species of Aleutian Geese. Unique among the various geese populations Semidi birds spend most of their time on the water, but every morning just before dawn they take flight to feed on pasture land owned by a local dairy farmer. See how biologists and farmers are working to protect this rare bird.
A leisurely trip through south central Oregon's Klamath Basin as seen through the eyes of our videographers.

Modern technology is making it possible to explore deep water reefs 150 feet below the surface and three miles from shore. Follow a team from Oregon State University as they deploy a remote-controlled underwater vehicle equipped with a camera to check on fish populations.

The Beer brothers have been logging their family's tree farm in the Indian Creek Valley for years. Oregon Field Guide joins the brothers as they take us on a tour of the family farm. The brothers are just a few of the family loggers left in Oregon as the big corporations have dominated the forest in recent years. Highlights of the tour include some antique logging equipment as well as a look at one of Oregon's largest black walnut trees.

The mountainŌĆÖs south side is one of the most heavily used recreation sites in the Pacific Northwest. But tempers are flaring between snowmobilers, hikers and skiers / Mt. St. Helens snow mobiles skiing skiers snow forest

Tiny, mouse-like tree voles live high up in the Douglas firs of Oregon and California. They are crucial to the forest ecosystem, as a primary food source for spotted owls and other predators. Researchers must climb high up into old growth trees to get a glimpse of the elusive vole.

Sea otters lived along Oregon's coast for thousands of years until fur traders wiped them out. But they may be on their way back. Track the history and plight of the sea otter along the coastal waters from California to Alaska. Follow a research crew in Neah Bay, WA. View spectacular underwater footage of the otters and their environment. Learn more from biologists about the hope for the otter's survival as a species and the obstacles it faces.
Traveling by dogsled and skis, we follow hearty adventurers to the remote Broken Top Mountain in the heart of the Three SisterŌĆÖs Wilderness / snow skiing climbing snow board scenery mountains
Logging has radically changed the ecosystem of central Oregon's high Cascades. As the land becomes more hospitable to squirrels chipmunks, it becomes hostile to native whiteheaded woodpeckers.

Until now, the canyon was hidden in the dark world of the deep ocean. But new technology is making it possible for modern day explorers to follow the crumbling landscape of a canyon that is actually an extension of the Columbia River and runs miles into the deep Pacific Ocean floor study oceanography

Crater Lake's iconic whitebark pine trees have been dying for decades and park ecologists estimate that they will be wiped out within 50 years. Blister rust, a fungus introduced from Europe over 100 years ago, has infected trees throughout the west coast region. Although the future looks bleak, scientists are trying to figure out methods for protecting the trees from this disease.

Portland is becoming known for new techniques in environmentally sustainable building designs. As the Ecotrust Building emerges from the rubble of an old warehouse, a number of green building techniques are demonstrated.
Wild coyotes thrive in and near urban areas. They appear at the Portland Airport and in Portland's suburban areas. Are they dangerous?
Explore forgotten parts of the Willamette on a five day, leisurely paddle trip down the river. Learn a bit of history and ecology about the river while enjoying the scenery.

Smith Rock near Madras Oregon has become the United Nations of Sport Climbing, because it attracts so many climbers from across the globe. Oregon native Alan Watts has done more than anyone to call attention to Smith Rock. He has helped develop the sport and mapped out most of the climbs which make Smith Rock a climbers paradise.

We head deep into the Wallowa wilderness. It's a long, grueling trip. It's all worth it for the chance to speed down the vertical paradise of the Eagle Cap Wilderness and stay in huts recently set up so the adventurous few can get a break from the weather.

Mosquitos pose potential health risks to humans. The Oregon Health Department is particularly concerned about the transmission of West Nile Virus and Western Decline Encephalitis. By sampling the mosquito population for these viruses, scientists may be able to give advanced warnings of a potential outbreak. Look at the latest efforts to try and control the mosquito population by treating pools of still water where mosquito larva live and grow.

Join researchers trying to find out more about the apprehensive Sandhill Crane which spend part of their winter near Portland's Columbia River wetlands. No one knows for sure where this group of Sandhills comes from - maybe Alaska or perhaps as far away as Siberia. Researchers hope to attach GPS tracking devices to a few to find out exactly where their breeding ground is.

Looking for some winter fun, why not try Skijoring - it's fast, fun and catching on. Joring means to drive, and with a couple of good dogs and a pair of cross country skies you too could hit the trails for some unique winter fun. The Cascade Skijoring Alliance was formed to bring a greater awareness of the sport and also to host a few competitions.

Shortly after Field Guide did a story about the Chetco River, the massive Biscuit Fire swept through the region. The Chetco River Canyon is a changed place, although some of the landscape was barely touched and many trees survived.

In what has to be one of Field Guide's toughest treks yet, Alan Wilson leads us by horse, foot, and kayak to see the pristine upper headwaters of the Chetco River in the heart of the Kalmiopsis wilderness. We reach the Chetco after an eight mile hike, then we paddle twenty miles down the rugged stream to our takeout.

We pay tribute to William Finley, a man who made incredible contributions to the future of Oregon's wildlife and natural resources. In the early 1900's Finley and his colleague Herman Bohlman went on photographic expeditions throughout Oregon. Finley was a dramatic speaker and he used his slides and movies to convince President Theodore Roosevelt to set aside lands (such as Klamath Basin and Three Arch Rocks) as national wildlife refuges.

You'll be amazed at some of the hidden wonders in the coast range as we hit the trails in search of little known waterfalls. Hike Kentucky Creek to where the Smith River and Kentucky Falls form the rarely seen double falls. The coast range is rich in waterfalls: Devil's Staircase, Sweet Creek, and Niagara are all explored. Also watch as scientists tell the story of Oregon's falls from a geological perspective.

A beautiful look at the scenery and wildlife of the Malheur National Wildlife Refuge, an oasis in the desert of southeastern Oregon. We hear the wind as well as bird calls as we look at photographs of beautiful cloud formations, a gorgeous sunset, and peaceful marshes that are home to such species as white pelican, red winged blackbird, and northern harrier.

If you haven't been hiking, camping, or boating on Oregon's public land lately then you may be in for a big surprise. What was once free now comes with a user fee and that has a lot of people upset. Learn about the controversy surrounding Oregon's new public land use fee laws.

We head deep into the back country of the Warner Uplands with archaeologists who have spent the last thirty years tracking down evidence of ancient civilizations. Petroglyphs, or images carved into rocks, have been found in the Warner Uplands, telling the untold stories of families that prospered here for thousands of years.

Animals avoid us, but Michael Durham has invented a way to capture them on film. He buried motion-detecting camera traps deep in the woods, and leaves them there for weeks or months at a time. His patience pays off with rare candid scenes of animals at home.

The Bend Badlands, just 15 miles from the growing population of Bend is suffering from abuse. Take a tour with Alice Elshoff of the Oregon Natural Desert Association and local rancer, Ray Clarno as they point out the destruction of petroglyphs, graffiti, the cutting down of ancient junipers and illegal dumping. The area, rich in geologic features is part of a BLM study area, but there is a strong push to get it designated as a wilderness area.

For almost 6 weeks a huge section of the Oregon coast turned deadly. A catastrophic drop in the oxygen levels of the water resulted in what scientists call a dead zone. Overpopulation caused a massive die off of microscopic algae in this area. Algae corpses sucked up all of the oxygen in the water and kicked off a domino effect. Scientists use modern technology to determine what exactly caused the algae to multiply in the first place.

The sewers of Portland are designed to transfer both household wastewater and rainwater to a treatment system. But when the rainfall is too heavy, the pipes can't handle the flow, and rainwater and untreated sewage are dumped directly into the Willamette River.

Invading species such as the mitten crab are hitchhiking around the globe on ocean-going cargo ships before landing in Oregon and seriously impacting the native environment. The creatures get picked up in the ballast water that is key to the stabilization and navigation of these giant vessels. In an effort to stop future invasions, aquatic ecologist Mark Sytsma, heads a program to test cargo ships visiting the Columbia River.

Oregon is now home to one of the largest dairies in the country. The gigantic Threemile Canyon Farm Dairy, build to supply the needs of Tillamook Cheese company, has 20,000 cows. Located in the high desert, it is being hailed as an environmental model for the dairy business.

Over 100 disabled students from 12 schools in the Portland area compete in the Special Olympics. Karen Morley, a P.E. teacher from Clackamas County, coordinated a massive volunteer force to keep the annual event going after budget cuts closed the local Special Olympics office.
South East Oregon

In a remote section of Oregon, it's all for one when a rancher needs a little help branding. Cooperation and sharing work is a necessity for these small cattle ranches. Friends and neighbors gather to get the job done and have some fun using their buckaroo skills of roping and riding.

Meet a group of adventure-seekers in Bend, Oregon who have found a challenging new way to play outdoors: mountain unicycling. A pair of bike builders in Bend adapted the traditional unicycle to more rugged terrain adding fatter tires, softer seats, and longer cranks. The result was the creation of a new twist on mountain biking that is gaining popularity among those willing to try the next new outdoor adventure.

Dr. Jane Lubchenco, Professor of Zoology from Oregon State University guides us on a fascinating look at the creatures that cling to life in the tide pools along the Oregon coast. See how the creatures of the tide pools --- mussels, sea stars, sea urchins and enenomies -- survive and reproduce in this underwater world.

A fascinating look at how insects like the lady bug and the praying mantis are excellent alternatives to using harmful chemicals in the control of pests. Beneficial insects are becoming increasingly popular in modern garden maintenance. Places like farmers markets have begun growing and selling these "good bugs", and more and more people are buying!

The lush 22-acre Crystal Springs Canyon that bisects Portland's Reed College campus stands today as a true urban oasis. It's all thanks to recent volunteer efforts by Reed's staff, students, and neighbors who have transformed the canyon from an overgrown weed patch to a thriving micro-ecosystem and urban watershed. The canyon now sports restored salmon habitat, revitalized trails and renewed wetlands.

Despite years of protection for Oregon's spotted owls, researchers have found little evidence of recovery. They have discovered that a new species of owl, the eastern barred owl, has moved into the protected habitat areas and is competing with the spotted owl for limited resources. The new invader is having a dramatic impact on the endangered species' survival.

As more people live and visit Central Oregon, the greater the impact is on the land. Join a BLM ranger to observe the problems he encounters on his rounds. With only three rangers to patrol the Bend/Prineville area, it's becoming more and more difficult to control illegal squatters, tree cutters, garbage dumpers and even methamphetamine labs that are springing up and threatening this beautiful natural resource.

This is your chance to head to the rugged terrain of central Oregon's Glass Buttes and see what it's like to try and live like a caveman or woman. The primitive camp out known as the Knap In is highlighted. Knapping is the term used for ancient stone working techniques. Glass butte is explored, for it's rare obsidian. Ancient weapons are recreated and tested.

Listen to the wind blow and to the calls of insects, coyotes, and birds in the Painted Hills Unit of the John Day Fossil Beds in central Oregon. Take in the beautiful visual record of wildflowers in bloom in various shades of yellow, purple, white, and blue. Photographs include scenic vistas, rock formations, canyon walls, and the John Day River.
We join a team of archaeologists and students as they search for proof that man lived in caves near central Oregon's Summer Lake during the ice age.
Studies are under way to determine how to reduce car wrecks and make cars safer. At the same time, the state of Oregon is using new technology to make traffic flow more smoothly and safely.

University of Oregon professor Kent Stevens explains that previous notions of various dinosaurs' postures and movements were based on inaccurate readings of fossil bones. Warped by time and impossibly heavy, bones alone give scientists false impressions of how dinosaurs were shaped and how they moved. Thanks to new digital computer technology created by Stevens, we now have a much better idea of just how dinosaurs really lived.

New technology helps scientists study the violent forces at work at the mouth of the Columbia River. Underwater sensors are helping to create a computer simulation of the currents that run from the Columbia River estuary, to the mouth of the river, and out to sea.

Ferguson Ridge is a small, volunteer run ski area tucked at the base of the Wallowa Mountains in northeastern Oregon. Created by locals in the 1970's, it's where area ranchers and their families come to experience fresh powder and visit with friends. Many of the kids here have grown up skiing and snowboarding with several making it into national competitions.

Dr. Cynthia Suchman conducts jellyfish research at the Hatfield Marine Science Center in Newport, Oregon. Suchman focuses her research on the Sea Nettle Jellyfish which lives off the Oregon Coast. There is concern that this predator is competing with local fish populations for the same food sources.
Hike through the high desert of the Pueblo Mountains.

This highly specialized extreme sport involves a squirt boat, which looks similar to a kayak. This small, sleek craft enables boaters to paddle on top of, as well as beneath the water's surface. We travel to the lower Clackamas River to watch some squirt boaters in action. Here we see the so-called 'Mystery Move' that allows the boaters to twirl and paddle underwater.

The Biscuit fire of 2002 was the state's largest recorded wildfire, burning 500,000 acres of Oregon's Siskiyou Mountains. Using archival footage we examine the area as it looked before and after the fire, and tackle the inevitable questions: When is fire good? How should forests be managed to keep fire from getting out of control?

Pure mountain spring water gushes from the ground high up in the Cascade Mountains. Forest Service hydrologist, Gordon Grant, studies these springs which originate from ancient underground resevoirs. The resevoirs are fed by winter snowfall that melts and percolates through the rock and soil. Eventually these frigid springs feed into the Willamette River a hundred miles away.

A precarious glacier in the crater of Mt. Saint Helens is growing at an unprecedented rate, posing potential danger to the valley below. We get an exclusive and rare visit into the crater itself and trek the moonscape of glaciers, boiling rivers and steam vents with a group of adventuresome scientists.

Scientists don't know why some cutthroat trout remain in their native streams, while others head out to sea, then return home to spawn. They also don't know why the ocean-going cutthroats have been failing to return in recent years. Biologists fit the trout with radio tags to find out.

Until recently, getting power from alternative sources such as wind, solar or methane gas was just too expensive to be done on a large scale. New technology is changing that, as this survey of a variety of power sources demonstrates.

More non-lethal methods of controlling predators are available today than ever, but some people are not interested in a peaceful coexistence. Electric fences and locking boxes keep bears out, and flagged fence-lines and noisy, radioactive guard boxes keeps wolves at bay, but are they cost effective for struggling ranchers? Explore with us the alternatives to shooting predators and learn about the controversy surrounding non-lethal methods.

At the expense of much time and sleep, biologists scour the Klamath marshes looking for Yellow Rails, a bird which is so elusive and so few in number that humans really know very little about them. Searching through the thick marsh grasses, biologists hope to not only spot Yellow Rails, but also catch them in order to measure and tag the birds for further research.

Injecting a giraffe for arthritis pain, weighing a Siberian tiger, taking x-rays of a blue-tongued skink lizard, testing the kidney function of a debrazis monkey, and applying medicine to the eye of a 1000 pound polar bear... Just an average work day for the veterinarian team at the Oregon Zoo. Watch these brave, dedicated animal lovers work as they face the challenges of one particularly busy day at the zoo.

While there are no waves or currents in the large exhibition tanks at the Oregon Coast Aquarium, it's still a challenging environment in which to dive. Professionals and a team of volunteers fend off sharks, eels and a giant Pacific octopus as they clean and maintain the underwater environment, and during visiting hours, they even become part of the exhibit.

Continuing his exploration of Oregon's natural wonders Nick Fisher returns to the Oregon Coast with this short film of Cascade Head. Once again Nick captures the sights and sounds of Oregon's natural beauty. Panoramic views, and wide open skies abound in this detailed exploration. Your sense of time will disappear into the deep blue sea and evergreen coast range.

The pacific lamprey, native to the Northwest, is so primitive that it's ancestors are among the earliest species in the fossil record. The species originated almost 350 million years ago. This unlovely fish was ignored by researchers in favor of game fish such as Trout and Salmon. Researchers near Cedar Creek are now looking more closely at the Lamprey as it's life cycle mimics that of many Salmon.

Steve Amen takes us to a special track meet that isn't about coming in first - it's about making it across the finish line against all odds. Planning, fund raising, and organizing donations and volunteers, the staff at Clackamas ESD work hard to put on this event every year.

Lewis and Clark saw many California Condors in Oregon. But pushed to the brink of extinction, these birds have been absent from this area for a century. Now an Oregon Zoo breeding program in Clackamas County will raise and reintroduce California Condors to the Grand Canyon, California and Southern Oregon. This means that one day this great bird could return to its ancient Columbia Gorge territory.

One of Oregon's best-known artists, Frank Boyden turns the magic and meaning he sees within nature into his artwork. Where the Salmon River empties into the Pacific Ocean under Cascade Head, Frank gleans inspiration for his printmaking and ceramic work. His art celebrates this rich landscape and reflects upon the ways nature can illuminate the human condition.

Elijah Mack of Eugene is a pioneer of river surfing, the newest extreme sport to hit Oregon. Founder of the World River Surfing Association, he says this new take on surfing brings a dimension of accessibility to the sport, since you no longer need to live near an ocean to jump on a board and ride the waves. Watch Elijah in action, surfing on the McKenzie River.
Join us for a thrilling ride on one manŌĆÖs personal railroad through some beautiful scenery.

Join a group of modern-day explorers as they attempt to run Falls Creek in Washington. These exploratory kayakers take the sport to the extreme and are willing to do all the work necessary, including carrying and dragging their 35-pound kayaks for miles through forests, for the ultimate prize of successfully pulling off a first descent.

Marmot Dam on the Sandy River near Mount Hood will be gone soon. It is cheaper to remove the dam than maintain it. Removing the dam will unleash tons sediment and a host of uncertainties. No one knows exactly what effect the removal of a dam this size will have on the river.

Visitors to Fort Stevens State Park might be surprised to learn that there's more than first meets the eye here. Situated below the concrete gun batteries that dot the coastline is a series of stairways and walkways that, for decades, played an important role in this country's great wall of defense.

Everett Mezentine is an 87-year-old who puts in 12-14 hour days farming. When that's done, he makes horse-drawn wagons. This Wamic farmer is one of the last craftsmen of carriages and wagons in Oregon. He does it all by hand, even harvesting the trees for the wood from his own land. The entire process takes years and his wagons aren't cheap, but the orders keep coming.

For experienced ice climbers in the Cascades, just getting to the ice is part of the adventure. The relative warmth of Pacific Northwest winters means that the sheer, vertical sheets of ice that climbers seek out are always changing.

Follow BPA linemen as they head into remote areas to climb massive towers for much needed repair work to keep the electricity flowing. Watch as two linemen replace an aviation light on a 200 foot tower.

Only three Oregon State troopers use boats, ski mobiles, horses and foot power to patrol 3,500 square miles of rugged backcountry in Wallowa County. Officers enforce Fish and Game violations on the Snake River, and investigate archaeological crime. Officers also check in on historic cabins. Watch as officers raft the Snake Rivers rapids in Hell's Canyon.

Scientists believe learning more about the common songbird can helps us gain a better understanding of the human brain. In Washington County, molecular biologist Claudio Mello is studying sparrows in the breeding season. The sparrows can learn and repeat five or six different songs, and recognize the songs of their rivals.

For centuries, the Columbia River was the region's highway. Now kayakers and canoers want to designate a "river trail" on the lower Columbia. The trail is 146 miles long and runs from Bonneville Dam to Astoria and the Pacific Ocean.

Tipped off by a chance spotting from a passerby, waterfall hunters set out on an exciting trek through the forest surrounding Mount Hood for a supposed waterfall that is neither mapped nor named. Over steep cliffs, slippery hillsides, and through thick tree cover, join us for an amazing discovery as we locate a secret waterfall of spectacular proportions.

Using similar techniques and equipment to snowboarding, sandboarding takes place on sand dunes rather than snow-covered mountains. The dunes of the Oregon coast, which can reach over 200 feet high, are perfect for a pair of local sandboarders.

In Christmas Valley, one TV tower engineer demonstrates his dedication to providing a communication link for remote farming communities. A group of workers from Radio Tower Company and Custom Metal Work ascends 900 feet up the OPB tower in Portland for repairs. Adverse weather, radiation exposure and gravity's pull, are all potential hazards these brave engineers must safeguard against.

The last documented wolverine sighting in Oregon was over 14 years ago, and people are hard at work to see if this reclusive animal still inhabits the state. Southern Cascade Wolverine Survey biologists visit avalanche-prone areas around Crater Lake in search of wolverine. And the Forest Service works with the volunteer group Cascadia Wild to track the animal in the Mt. Hood National Forest.

Until fairly recently, we hadn't seen a wolf in Oregon in over 60 years. Now, biologists say more wolves are coming and that's creating quite a controversy. This Field Guide special tracks the wolf in Idaho and explores the many issues facing Oregon as we prepare for the return of the West's most controversial animal.

Commercial gillnet fishing is a common method of harvesting salmon in Alaska, British Columbia, Washington, and Oregon. When the gear is lost at sea, it becomes a ghost net that continues to trap and kill sea birds and marine life, including endangered salmon. Field Guide follows net recovery efforts, including one activist who uses an underwater video camera to locate lost nets.

The American White Pelican is a very large white bird with black wing tips and a long, wide orange bill. They are graceful in flight, moving their wings in slow powerful strokes. White Pelicans nest in colonies of several hundred pairs on islands in remote brackish and freshwater lakes of inland North America. Shooting by poachers is the largest known cause of mortality, although the species is protected by the Migratory Bird Treaty Act of 1918.
The deer mice population is on the increase and that could be trouble for people because they harbor a potentially fatal virus.

Oregon scientists are hoping to learn more about the behaviors of the various flat fish in hopes of better managing their populations. The marine science center at Newport is helping scientist mimic ocean conditions to better understand flat fish behaviors. Newport scientist's have learned that structures on the ocean floor are vital for juvenile flat fish development.
From water to woods to sky, Field Guide explores the Klamath Wildlife Refuge.

Head back in time to central Oregon's Fort Rock Basin to learn more about the areas first human inhabitants and their ancient life. Archeologist study obsidian quarries and track the movements of Oregon's Native American tribes.

With the invasion of Iraq, Oregon National Guard members must be prepared to face urban combat. Leschi Town is a new training ground at Fort Lewis that is designed to train soldiers how to maneuver city streets and buildings.
Surfing on a river, surfing on sand, unicycle mountain biking, underwater kayaking, para-sail ocean slaloming... check out these extreme and slightly bizarre modern sports in a wild photo-essay.

It's underwater "reality T.V." as scientists use a complex system of cameras to study vulnerable juvenile salmon in Siletz Bay. It's a particularly sensitive area for the young salmon, as this inlet is where they make the transition from freshwater to saltwater fish.

The mudflats of the Willapa Bay Wildlife Refuge in SW Washington bay are a beautiful feeding ground for shorebirds. But in recent decades, spartina, an invasive grass from the east coast, has established itself in the bay, damaging the feeding grounds and threatening the ecosystem. Land managers have adopted an aggressive and controversial eradication program, using powerful herbicides to restore the bay's habitat.

As chemicals threaten the soil and cause harmful agricultural runoff into rivers, many Oregon vineyards have turned towards organic methods of growing grapes. Although it is a much more difficult way to farm, organic growers take pride in their final product. Sustainable vineyards must be certified in order to call themselves organic, but with over nine different types of certifications, questions remain about whether or not buyers actually care.

50,000 years ago Mt. Hood was entirely buried under glacial ice. Today glaciers throughout the United States are in rapid decline, taking with them essential water resources. Fountain and Percy set out on foot to study the current state of Elliot glacier on Mt. Hood in order to provide valuable information for developing computer technology that will be able to accurately measure the size and movement of glacier ice.

The rugged, but vulnerable Obsidian Trail in the heart of the Three Sisters Wilderness Area is in danger from both over use and climate change. Born from volcanic upheaval this wilderness may look impregnable, but its soils and vegetation are fragile. The Forest Service has restricted its use over the past fifteen years which has helped to limit the impact from hikers and campers. But Collier Glacier's rapid retreat is cause for concern.

The Illinois river in Southwest Oregon is one of the most dangerous rivers in the state. With class 3/4 rapids, one right after another, only experienced rafters should navigate this whitewater. Treacherous passages like the "green wall" test rafters' skill and bravery. But with scenic shorelines and peaceful surroundings, these expert guides show us how the Illinois, when taken seriously, can be enjoyed as one of Oregon's most beautiful rivers.
Knappton Cove on the Columbia River was once an entry point for immigrants coming to the United States around the turn of the century. Astoria

This prairie of rolling hills and grasslands is home to a wide variety of animals, but this valuable ecosystem is now in danger from development. Dr. Pat Kennedy an OSU avian ecologist is your guide to the wildlife and dangers which threaten the Zumwalt. Dr. Kennedy studies the many bird which live in the Zumwalt such as Western Meadowlarks, Savannah sparrows, Brewers Blackbirds and Red Tailed Hawks.

Meth labs in Oregon are moving out of urban areas and in to out-of-the-way places like forests where the huge quantities of contaminants that are generated now threaten the environment. Criminals also threaten natural resources by stealing old-growth trees, and public property like bridge rails. Follow a group of specialists as they clean up a creek in the foothills of Elk Horn range, where toxic chemicals used to manufacture meth were dumped.

At the Oregon Climate Center in Corvallis, a climatologist explains how the state's weather varies from region to region and looks at some of the weather disasters Oregon has suffered over the decades.

There are no ski areas in the Wallowa Mountains of Eastern Oregon. So to ski some the finest powder in the Western U.S., folks have to be willing to hike a long ways. Join a group of adventurous skiers who are up to the task, especially since there's a cozy yurt camp waiting for them at the end of the day.

Erik Verdouw and Patricia Kortekaas of Eugene Oregon, have been treating people for years, but have also turned their attention to animals. Their business Full Spectrum Therapy specializes in manual therapy for both humans and animals. Seen as a compliment to more traditional veterinarian techniques, this therapy is less evasive and often a first step to avoid costly medical treatments.

Each year, scores of hikers and hunters become lost or disappear in the wilderness. Search and rescue dogs can be one of the most valuable tools rescue teams can have in finding these people. Certain breeds of dogs excel at this ability, but it takes a lot of training to refine and direct this skill. Attend a "scent" seminar to learn how dogs can be trained to pick up scents and follow them to the source.

Join us for dinner in the woods as we show you how to go grocery shopping in the great outdoors. John Kallas demonstrates how fun and nutritious it is to collect wild foods, including gapers, mahogany clams, cockles, thimbleberries, ladyfern, Japanese knotweed, cow parsnips, and even Himalayan blackberry shoots.

Trying to manage growth and avoid sprawl is a critical role of the Metropolitan Regional Planning Agency (Metro). The agency was established in the late '70s to assure Portland wouldn't suffer the blighted landscapes and traffic gridlock of cities like Houston or Atlanta. In this half hour special producer Jim Newman takes a critical look at some of the projects Metro is working on, including the city of Damascus.

Painful, thrilling, crazy -- these are just some of the terms freeriders themselves use to describe their sport. Freeriding is a new twist on a style of mountain biking that originated in Canada where wooden ramps and bridges and logs were laid so bikers could avoid muddy forest trails. Join a group of these daring thrill-seekers in the Oregon forests as they negotiate trails over four-inch logs, high platforms, and bone-rattling jumps.

In 1996 the pronghorn were in trouble at Hart Mountain Antelope Refuge. Biologists estimated that coyotes were killing 80% of fawns and a plan to kill the coyotes was considered. Instead, livestock were removed from the refuge, allowing meadows and streamside vegetation to improve the forage for pronghorn. More favorable weather has also allowed the pronghorn to flourish. Now there are more than ever - some 2,400 - on the 280,000-acre refuge.

There may be as many as a billion burrowing shrimp in the 900 acres of tide flats at Yaquina Bay. But scientists studying the creature and the role it plays in tempering pollution believe that more than 50 percent of its population may be infected with a lethal parasite. The parasite, which may have arrived relatively recently in the holds of cargo ships from China, reduces the shrimp's capacity for reproduction.

A small park in Benton County is home to the largest population of one of the rarest butterflies in the world. Until recently little was known about the Taylor's Checkerspot butterfly, but studies now confirm their numbers are declining. They need a specialized prairie meadow habitat to survive and that habitat is disappearing. See how the Oregon Zoo and some private landowners are trying to bring hope to the survival of this beautiful creature.

18 years ago in Chiloquin, Oregon, Quenten Breen began construction on Train Mountain, a place where grown men could go to ride small trains. Today, over 24 miles of track lies in this scenic countryside, making the Guinness Book of World Records' longest hobby railroad in the world. See some of the meticulous reproductions, one-eighth the size of normal trains, and learn what future plans are for in store for this railroad fantasy land.

Travel to the past through the massive historic photo collection at the University of Oregon's Knight Library. There are about 500,000 images in the collection, 40,000 of which are glass-plate negatives. Thanks to today's computer processors, the crisply clear glass plates can be scanned and blown up 400-500 percent, revealing astonishing details. We'll have a look at Pioneer Square circa 1910, inside a schoolroom in 1905, and more.

The ocean seems like a single static mass of water but actually it functions more like the atmosphere with eddies and currents and a circulation system that is not fully mapped. Oregon State University scientists have exciting new tools which are allowing them to track the complexities of ocean circulation. Pacific ocean

In the vast sagebrush prairies of Eastern Oregon, the jackrabbit population is booming. In Eastern Washington, however, loss of sagebrush habitat has contributed to jackrabbit decline. Field Guide examines the jackrabbit's role as part of a tightly woven food chain, affecting the presence of other animals including coyotes, pronghorn and hawks, and the damage they can cause to farmers' crops.

An old sheep ranch near Riddle Mountain, south of Burns, has been in Dick Jenkins' family for over a century. He's proud of the fact that five generations have lived on this land and eager to share the history and beauty of the area with others. Jenkins shows visitors the abandoned buildings, land scattered with artifacts, and the collection of photographs taken by his grandmother over a period of 40 years.
The John Day Fossil Beds are a treasure trove of fossils from millions of years ago.

This half hour special uncovers new volcanic mysteries as we take you trekking along with scientists on Mount Saint Helens and in the Oregon Cascades to discover what we've learned and what we still don't know about the 'Volcanoes in Our Backyard.' Scientists also search for clues that may help them predict the next eruption in the cascades. In October 2004, almost 25 years after Mount Saint Helens blew 1,300 feet off its top, the volcano again erupted. It was a small eruption compared to the cataclysmic blow of 1980, but it made clear that the mountain was coming back to life. But Mount Saint Helens isn't the only volcano in the Northwest showing signs of life.

60 years ago, in the Pueblo Mountains, near the Oregon/Nevada border town of Denio, a B-24 crashed killing 11 U.S. Army airmen. Scattered wreckage over an area dubbed "Propeller Meadows" remains as a memorial to the 11 lives lost that night. Hitting the mountain at full speed, during clear weather, with no distress call from the pilot, the unexplainable plane crash remains a mystery to this day. Locals share their feelings about the accident.

As many as 50,000 petrels nest in burrows on Saddle Rock Island off Crook Point, making them hard to reach and hard to study. They fly at night to avoid their primary predator, the seagull, and often range 200 miles out to sea in search of food for their young. But thanks to some dedicated U of O marine biologists, were learning more about a tiny bird and the amazing amount of energy it puts into raising its offspring.

Hop aboard one of Oregon's only regularly operating full-sized steam locomotives for a historic tour through the beautiful Sumpter Valley. It's a slow, smoky, noisy ride, taking passengers back in time to experience the sights, sounds and smells of train travel in the 1890s. The meticulously restored engines hearken to a time when Sumpter was the second largest city in Oregon, boasting a large timber and gold trade.

The wild and scenic Rogue River attracts rafters, jet boaters, fisherman and others in increasing numbers, and sometimes these groups are at odds as to how the river should be used. But new regulations and more aggressive enforcement by the Bureau of Land Management (BLM) and the Forest Service are beginning to turn the tide on problems. Permits are being enforced, jet boat traffic is limited, and bears are kept at bay with cleaner campsites.

Some say it's a transforming experience to climb 140 feet into the treetops, via a system of ropes and ascenders, to a hammock-like platform to enjoy the view from a very different vantage point. Rob Miron of the Pacific Tree climbing Institute guides you up and through the canopy. Old growth trees, some as large as 300 feet are explored. You will also see some of the unique plants and wildlife which thrive high above the forest floor.

Roads and highways help people get around, but for wild animals they can do the opposite. Every year 1.5 million animal/vehicle collisions occur on U.S. roads, killing over 200 people and countless animals. Some species are being fragmented into pockets of habitat too small to support long-term survival. Field Guild looks at methods that have been tried in Oregon and elsewhere in the world to help animals move as their lifecycle demands.

Trouble is brewing in Oregon's southwest coastal forests in the form of Phytophthora ramorum, a deadly microbial pest that could devastate woodlands throughout the state. Oregon considers the threat from Sudden Oak Death so serious that they are taking drastic measures in response to each positive sighting. If Sudden Oak Death goes unchecked, it could cause significant damage to Oregon's forest ecosystems.

An area at the confluence of the Columbia and Willamette rivers was once home to a thriving Native American village. Hidden underground in a section of the Ridgefield National Wildlife Refuge near Vancouver are clues to an ancient culture that occupied the land from around 1450 to 1835. Lewis and Clark were greeted by villagers when they visited the area 200 years ago. Excavations in the 1990s uncovered over 10,000 artifacts.

Most people can't put the concept of kayaking and fishing together. But for one Oregon couple, it's the only way to fish. Field Guide journeys to the waters off the beautiful Beverly Beach to see how these two kayak fishermen can get into areas that big boats can't and catch prized lingcod.

Some amazing new technology may make it possible to track individual salmon in the open ocean for the first time. Survival rates for salmon at sea have been dropping, and scientists hope to learn why.

Over the last decade, Oregon has become one of the best-known skateboarding destinations in the world, due primarily to the proliferation of public and private skateboard parks throughout the state. The first and most legendary is the Burnside skate park in Portland, a DIY monument to skating. Meet some of the modern-day pioneers who turned Oregon into a skateboarding mecca.

New technological advances may make it possible for wave-generated electricity to become a viable alternative energy source. Just off Newport a collaboration between OSU and Columbia Power Technologies is underway. A power generating buoy has gone from the safety of the lab to real world testing on the open water. In the future these buoy's could be supplying energy to Oregon homes and business.

Dr. Jason Dunham of US Geological Survey studies the rare Western Pearl Shell mussel in Oregon's Steamboat Creek. The Pearl Shell can live for up to 200 years.
