Educational Recreational Adventures takes students from the Muslim Educational Trust, whose family roots trace to Africa and the Middle East, to Snow School.

26-miles of clear lakes, dramatic waterfalls and majestic old-growth forests earn this Oregon river corridor the nickname ŌĆ£AmericaŌĆÖs #1 Trail.ŌĆØ We check in with hikers and bikers, as well as forest managers and a geologist to explore all things Trail.

Biologists learn the "how-to's" of wildlife capture and research. Where do aspiring wildlife graduate students go to learn their trade? Wildlife capture school! a dozen students spend weeks in the Alvord desert to learning the art of radio tracking ants, how to film the body mechanics of fleet-footed lizards, how to use drones and IR photography to observe wildlife and how to conduct good old fashioned radio tracking of desert wildlife.
Enter the colorful world of Chris CarvlahoŌĆÖs butterfly photography.

Every year a Portland graphic artist sets out to confuse people as much as possible. Peter Rockwell reveals his secrets on how he designs the annual corn maze at Kruger Farm on Sauvie Island. He does not use GPS. Rockwell prefers a low tech method that involves a pencil sketch, a scan by a computer, a massive enlargement, chalk and a tractor.┬Ā
A few Oregon balloonists make their own hot air balloons.

Mt Hood, Oregon has the distinction of being the only place in North America that can offer skiers the opportunity to train year-round. One camp - Mt Hood Summer Ski Camps - has been training tomorrow's stars of snow sports since the 1970s. We check in with the latest group of up-and-coming "campers" as they hone their skills on the summer slopes.

The March 27, 1964 "Good Friday" earthquake rocked Southcentral Alaska with 5 minutes of shaking. The magnitude 9.2 earthquake left communities in ruins and triggered a tsunami that traveled south at over 500 mph towards Oregon. When the tsunami struck Oregon and California hours later, it destroyed homes, flooded communities and killed 11 people. It remains the largest tsunami in U.S history. Since then, scientists have learned that the "big one" - an earthquake triggered a by a rupture in the Cascadia subduction zone just 50 miles offshore- frequently triggers tsunami exponentially larger than the one in 1964. New maps pinpoint just how far the devastation of such "local" tsunamis can reach- and the Japanese tsunami of 2011 offers up a glimpse of the destruction we face. But how much more prepared are we now than we were in 1964? tsunami, japan 2011, japanese tsunami, west coast tsunami, Gpod Friday earthquake, 1964 earthquake, Cascadia Subduction Zone, USGS. Seaside, Oregon and Crescent City, CA

The Reach and Treat Team trains every month to take professional paramedic skills into the wild. AMR provides advanced life support in the wilderness when medics join in rescues in the snow, dangling from ropes and in rushing rivers. rescue, paramedics, Reach and Treat, ambulance, climber, Mount Hood, Rocky Butte, Clackamas River

Eduardo "Eddy" Cartaya and a team of adventurers set out to explore a new cave system in one of the most dangerous landscapes in America-- the crater of Mount Saint Helens. Braving rockfall and avalanches, the team launches a 5-day expedition to be the first people ever to explore a cave system within a glacier that has grown up to 300 feet thick in places. Descending hundreds of feet through the ice, the expedition uncovers a never-before seen world of ice and steam and volcanic beauty - but what's causing these wild formations inside one of the country's only growing glaciers? volcano, glacier, caves, mount saint helens, Mt. St. Helens, Mt. Saint Helens, volcanims, volcanology, spelunking, caving, expedition caving, cascades, search and rescue, cascades volcano observatory.

An Oregon photographer who was just 8-years-old when the Wilderness Act passed celebrates 50 years of protecting public lands in a most personal way. Peter Marbach not only seeks out wilderness for his nature photography but he says it was vital to his own healing from emergency heart surgery. His favorite spot is an alpine meadow off tail and high above timberline on Mount Hood. wilderness, Mount Hood, photographer, alpine, meadow, wilderness act,

column4: 26.02 and 32.06 Tim Palmer: On Oregon's Rivers
column5: Tim Palmer has canoed, rafted and kayaked nearly every major river in Oregon. His Field Guide to Oregon rivers looks at the lesser known natural history features of these rivers.South Umqua, Chetco river, rafting, raft, author

column4: 26.02 Ranchers Act to Protect Sage Grouse
column5: Sage grouse, a desert bird that depends on sagebrush, has been in decline. The US government may add it to the endangered species list. Ranchers fear the grouse could become their spotted owl, restricting use of western lands. A number of groups are adopting conservation measures in hopes it will be enough to stop the listing as endangered.sage grouse, sagebrush, desert, endangered species, lek, ranch, ranching, barbed wire, fire, wildfire, southeastern Oregon

We tag along with shepherdess Briana Murphy as she wields her herd of brushing-eating goat from the sublime vineyards of the Willamette Valley to the industrial wastelands of NE Portland. Goats, brushing clearing, livestock, vineyard, shepherdess, shepherd

For nearly 50 years, residents of tiny Glide, Oregon (population 2,000, near Roseburg) have been collecting and showing their favorite wildflowers. We follow the collection, identification, display set-up and show of Oregon's loveliest wildflowers.Wildflower, Glide, botany, flower show, North Umpqua, southern Oregon

One group of Sandhill cranes skips the long migration to California and spends winter at Sauvie Island, near Portland. In summer, they head only to British Columbia, while other populations venture farther north to Alaska. This information was gleaned by researcher Gary Ivey after he put satellite trackers on the legs of cranes in Oregon and again in Alaska. sandhill cranes, Sauvie Island, Conboy Lake National Wildlife Refuge, Alaska, satellite tracking, banding, colt, chick

column4: 26.04 and 28.16 Urban Youth and the Zen of Flyfishing
column5: U.S. Navy veteran Chad Brown deals with his PTSD through fly-fishing. Now he's bringing the art of fly-fishing to urban youth who lack access to nature and have their own issues to deal with.U.S. Navy, fly-fishing, urban youth, fishing, Olympic National Park, disadvantaged youth, New Currents Outdoors, Soul Rivers

column4: 26.04 Queen Bee Project: Breeding Winter Survivors
column5: In a multi-year experiment, two beekeepers attempt to breed stronger bees, capable of surviving winter in Portland. Tim Wessels & Glen Andresen were tired of having to import bees from California and Hawaii to replenish their hives. We follow them for a year during which their colonies get slammed by a brutally cold winter. bees, honey bees, larvae, winter, hive, swarm, beekeeper

Ever wonder how those cool terrain parks get the shapes that allow snowboarders to execute spectacular jumps and death-defying flips? TheyŌĆÖre the meticulous work of Mt Bachelor's skilled team of snowcat operators. We ride along to see how itŌĆÖs done. Skiing, snow boarding, snowcat, snow-cat, sno-cat, terrain park, Mt Bachelor, sculpting

We track the ŌĆ£greenŌĆØ that noble fir boughs bring to our local economy as they are harvested from our national forests, fashioned into wreaths, garlands and swags and then shipped all over the country. Christmas, wreath, swag, tree, holiday, trees, greens, national forest, economics, forest, Gifford Pinchot national forest

column4: 26.05 Ospreys Rebound, Rely on Help from Humans
column5: Some welders, a photographer and a retired biologist have all had front row seats to watch birds that have found a way not only to live with, but thrive off of humans. Osprey, the original sea hawk, are doing fine these days. It wasnŌĆÖt always this way. Now osprey very much like what we build.osprey, birds, adaptation, manufacturing, crane, industry willamette Valley
Portland's weirdness comes out in an impromptu version of an 800 Norwegian ski race.

For more than a decade, a herd of elk has been hanging out in and around the up-scale coastal town of Gearhart, browsing the golf course and even taking refreshing dips in the surf. But as the herd is growing, so are concerns about safety and property destruction. We talk to the locals and wildlife experts about why the elk come here, whether theyŌĆÖre a nuisance or an opportunity to drawn even more tourists to the area.Elk, resource management, wildlife, coast
Find out what endurance riding is all about and why those who enjoy this equestrian sport jokingly wonder if it's a hobby or addiction. Horses, Horse, horse riding, Central Oregon, dusty, race

column4: 26.06 Oregon Families Collect Antlers Without Killing Deer or Elk
column5: In central Oregon there's an increasingly popular form of hunting that never kills animals. Kids and parents comb the ground in late winter searching for fallen antlers that deer naturally shed. Oregon Shed Hunters encourage responsible shed hunting in the hopes that the state leaves the sport unregulated. shed, hunting, antlers, deer, mule deer, central Oregon, near Bend

We enjoy them for the beauty, recreation and wildness. But many of our forests begin life in seed orchards. The seeds grown in Bureau of Land Management seed orchards furnish timber companies, restoration projects and landscapers across the world with stronger, faster growing trees as well as a sustainable income for the state. Douglas fir, seed, orchard, coast range, forestry, timber company, clear cut, timber industry, reforestation, agriculture, Lorane, Sweet Home, Canby

column4: 26.07 Hiker Discovers Railroad History Hidden in Columbia Gorge
column5: Despite its sheer cliffs, the Columbia River Gorge was home to a spider web of short railroads a century ago. They hauled out trees. Today, Don Nelsen, a passionate walking history-book-of-an-explorer bushwhacks off trail and finds hunks of that railroad history still lying in the forest on both the Oregon & Washington sides of the gorge. railroad, logging, hiking, trains, donkey engine, timber, rail, Larch Mountain, Table Mountain, Hamilton Mountain
A relaxing look at some wildlife on the Malheur refuge. Malheur, birds, deer, owl, cranes

The humongous fungus ranks as the single largest living organism in the world and it lives underground in eastern Oregon. This honey mushroom covers more than 3 square miles in the Malheur National Forest.

Sage Clegg folded her body against a tower of basalt and turned the GoPro on herself. The shade was a small defense against the blistering heat in the depth of OregonŌĆÖs Owyhee Canyonlands. ŌĆ£IŌĆÖm definitely on edge today. Not only have I almost stepped on nine rattlesnakes, itŌĆÖs also just really stressful travel,ŌĆØ Clegg said, tilting the camera down to her legs and revealing a field of flame-red prick marks rising in response to a brush with stinging nettle. ŌĆ£I really wish I wasnŌĆÖt alone right now.ŌĆØ CleggŌĆÖs nearly 800-mile trek on the Oregon Desert Trail in 2013 took its toll, but by the end of that summer, she had become the first person to complete the entire route. Her pioneering effort put OregonŌĆÖs newest long-distance trail on the map. And yet, today, only a small handful of others have successfully managed to repeat her accomplishment.

A sea creature normally found in the tropics has suddenly exploded in the ocean off the Oregon coast. Oregon is famous for its scenic waterfalls. In fact, its tallest waterfall, Multnomah Falls, is the most visited natural recreation site in the state, attracting more than 2 million sightseers a year. Yet, the largest waterfall in Oregon is perhaps its least visited. Even though itŌĆÖs just a few miles south of Portland, itŌĆÖs hardly a tourist destination. ThatŌĆÖs because for more than 150 years, Willamette Falls has been surrounded by massive industrial buildings, blocking it from public access. People who get a glimpse of the falls see it from the far cliffs of Oregon City. Few get close enough to it to feel the spray and hear the thunder of the water, which plummets from basalt cliffs four-stories tall. ItŌĆÖs the second largest waterfall in the U.S. by volume ŌĆö the Niagara of the West.┬Ā┬Ā
